entry.title: Currency Conversion in SAP Datasphere Using Graphical Views
entry.content:
Introduction: Currency Conversion in SAP

In SAP landscapes such as S/4HANA, SAP Datasphere, and SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC), handling multiple currencies is a fundamental requirement due to global operations and diverse organizational structures. Business transactions are usually posted in the local currency of the country where the transaction takes place, but reporting and analysis often require additional currency perspectives

SAP systems can support multiple organizational and reporting currencies, depending on system configuration and business requirements, such as:

• Company Code Currency -  The legal reporting currency used for statutory and financial reporting.

• Group (Global) Currency - Used for consolidation and group-level reporting across multiple company codes.

• Profit Center Currency - Enables profitability analysis when a dedicated profit center currency is configured.

• Cost Center Currency - Supports internal cost tracking and management accounting scenarios.

• Transaction Currency - The currency in which the original business transaction is posted.

• Controlling Area Currency - Used for management accounting and controlling across company codes.

• Document Currency - The currency stored at the accounting document level for audit and traceability.

• Functional Currency - Represents the primary currency of business operations, commonly used in management reporting.

To ensure accurate and comparable reporting across entities and regions, these currencies must be converted using well-defined exchange rates.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Currency conversion in SAP Datasphere plays a key role by:

  • Centralizing currency conversion logic
  • Enabling consistent currency handling across models
  • Providing harmonized financial data for analytics and planning in SAC

This makes SAP Datasphere the foundation for reliable multi-currency reporting in modern SAP analytics scenarios.

In SAP Datasphere, currency conversion can be implemented in Graphical Views, Analytical Models, and SQL Views, and can also be applied dynamically at runtime in SAP Analytics Cloud.

In the following post, I will be covering the currency conversion in graphical view with BW as a source system.

Prerequisite:

Based on the source system (BW, S4HANA etc) the ABAP connection should be configured from source to Datasphere system.

Step 1: Import TCUR* tables, views, data flows and remote tables into Datasphere Tenant

Go to Data Builder

Select '+' symbol

Select 'Currency Conversion Views'

Note: In the Source drop down list, based on the type of source different artifacts will be created in Datasphere.

Manual - Local tables and views

SAP S/4 HANA Cloud / On Premise - Local tables, views and data flows will be imported
SAP ABAP connection - Local tables, views, data flows and remote tables will be imported.

Select Connection as ABAP

By selecting ABAP as a source - Local tables, views, data flows and remote tables will be imported.

Click on 'Create' - All the objects will be imported in to Datasphere tenant, localize the objects in a folder to use globally for different spaces.

Note: Naming convention as follows by default, option available to change Business Name

Remote tables - SAP.CURRENCY.RTABLE.TCUR*

Data Flows - SAP.CURRENCY.DATAFLOW.TCUR*

Tables - SAP.CURRENCY.TABLE.TCUR*

Views - SAP.CURRENCY.VIEW.TCUR*

Once you’ve created the currency conversion views and their supporting objects, you can share the TCUR* tables with other spaces. The spaces to which you share these tables will then see your space as an alternative source in the Source field above.

Step 2: Make TCUR* data available from BW to SAP Datasphere

Since the connection type is ABAP with BW, the existing source tables in the BW system must be mapped to their corresponding remote tables in SAP Datasphere.

Select the Remote table which are imported automatically in the system.

Example: For TCURR configuration, the same steps must be repeated for all imported TCUR* tables.

Go to 'Data Builder'

Search 'SAP.CURRENCY.RTABLE.TCURR' table

Open the table and set up the 'Remote' connection details (BW_ABAP : configured ABAP connection from BW to Datasphere)

Note: There are different methods to replicate data into Remote table:

Start Data Replication: Fetches data from the source BW ABAP table (TCURR) into the corresponding remote table in SAP Datasphere and stages it. This step can be included in a task chain. (STAGED)

Enable Real-Time Replication: Reads data virtually from BW into SAP Datasphere on demand, without staging. (VIRTUAL)

Once this step is completed, go to 'Data Builder' -> search for data flow 'SAP.CURRENCY.DATAFLOW.TCURR' -> Select 'Run'

Note: The data flow can be scheduled to run at regular intervals or included in a task chain for daily execution.

Once the data flow is completed (which can be monitored using the icon below), the data can be viewed in the currency view SAP.CURRENCY.VIEW.TCURR, a default SQL-based view provided by SAP

SAP.CURRENCY.VIEW.TCURR (provided by SAP)

 

Step3: Currency conversion in Datasphere views.

Go to 'Data Builder' (select space if applicable)

Select 'New Graphical View' or select if any existing view available which requires currency conversion.

There are three columns:

'Sale_Amount_CompCode' the measure contains amount to be converted

 'Company_Code_Currency' holds the source currency

 'Profit_Center_Currency' holds the target currency

Go to View -> Select the node where currency conversion need to apply -> Add 'fx' step

Select 'fx' step -> Details -> select '+' -> select 'Currency Conversion Column'

Set the Element Properties

Provide Business Name, Technical Name, Data type and Decimal changes

Currency Properties

Source Amount Column - Represents the column which requires conversion in this case 'Sale_Amount_CompCode''

Source Currency - Currency works as a source value  in this case 'Company_Code_Currency'

Target currency - Currency works as a target value in this case 'Profit_Center_Currency'

Reference Date - This is Year/Date to be used as a reference during conversion.

Steps: Different options available and one of the key element

 

Shift

The amount is multiplied to remove decimals. Example 123.45 USD → 12345

Convert

The shifted amount is converted using the exchange rate. Example 12345 × exchange rate

Round

The converted value is rounded based on the target currency’s decimal rules.

Shift Back

The value is divided to restore the correct decimal places.

Note: For any simple currency conversion, recommended to select only 'Convert' and 'Round'

Conversion properties

Client - Client 000 is typically used for currency tables in SAP systems

Conversion Type - Exchange rate type maintained in TCURR (for example, M – Standard rate, P – Budget rate)

Error Handling - Defines the value to be generated if no valid exchange rate is found.

Advanced Properties

Lookup: Regular and Reverse (to be selected if reverse exchange rate is maintained in the system)

Accuracy: Compatibility (Matches classic SAP (ECC/BW) currency results) and Highest (Provides maximum precision for accurate financial reporting)

Date Format: Auto Detect (Automatically picks the correct date format from source data and applies the right exchange rate)

All remaining views relevant to TCUR* will get auto populated by default, as its configured at beginning.

Activate and Deploy the view

Result Output:

Exchange rate from AUD to GBP is maintained as 1.75 in BW system

Data From BW System:

Client

Exchange Type

From

To

Valid From

Exchange Rate

600

P

AUD

GBP

01.01.2025

/1.75

Values are converted using the applicable exchange rate and published in a new column called “Profit Center Amount.”

Conclusion: By centralizing currency conversion logic in SAP Datasphere, organizations can ensure consistent, auditable, and scalable multi-currency reporting across analytical and planning scenarios. This approach simplifies downstream consumption in SAP Analytics Cloud while maintaining alignment with SAP’s standard currency configuration.

 

Thank you for taking the time to read this article. I hope this overview helps you understand and implement currency conversion in SAP Datasphere more effectively. Your feedback, questions, and suggestions are welcome in the comments section.

 

Regards

Lokesh Kumar Pothapola

entry.title: Transitioning to the New China Cash Flow Statement Solution: What You Need to Know
entry.content:
As regulatory reporting requirements continue to evolve, SAP is committed to providing customers with modern, efficient solutions that meet compliance needs while leveraging the latest technology capabilities. For organizations operating in China, an important transition is underway regarding the Cash Flow Statement reporting solution.

Understanding the Evolution

The legacy China Cash Flow Statement solution (IDCNCASH) has served customers well over the years, but technological advancement necessitates modernization. Since S/4HANA CE2111/OP2021, SAP has offered an enhanced solution built on the SAP Document and Reporting Compliance (DRC) platform, which provides significant improvements across multiple dimensions.

Key Advantages of the New Solution

The DRC-based Cash Flow Statement addresses several limitations of the legacy approach:

  • Performance and Architecture: The new solution leverages SAP HANA's in-memory database capabilities, delivering superior performance even with large data volumes. Unlike the legacy solution, which faced timeout issues with substantial datasets, the modern architecture is designed for scalability.
  • Data Consistency: By sourcing data directly from the ACDOCA table, the new solution ensures alignment with core financial data and other related reports. This eliminates the data inconsistencies that occasionally occurred with the legacy solution's separate data sources.
  • Enhanced Currency Support: While the legacy solution supported only four currency types, the new solution accommodates all currency types assigned to company codes and ledgers in S/4HANA, providing greater flexibility for multinational operations.
  • Proactive Error Detection: The new solution determines offsetting accounts during document posting rather than at report runtime, enabling earlier identification and resolution of issues.
  • Advanced Functionality: Built on the DRC platform, the solution offers scheduled tasks, historical snapshots, analytical views, and customer extension capabilities—features that support more sophisticated reporting workflows.

Migration Timeline and Support

Starting with CE2602, SAP has introduced toggle functionality allowing users to switch between the legacy and new solutions, facilitating a gradual transition.

  • When users open the old version app, a dialog will promote the new version app with functional benefits, and allow a direct switch to the new version app (DRC: Run Statutory Reports).

  • In the new app (DRC), users can submit feedback on their experience and switch back to the old app at any time.

While SAP has extended the transition window to accommodate the large user base, customers are encouraged to plan their migration proactively.

Implementation Resources

SAP has developed comprehensive implementation materials to support the transition, including detailed white papers and video tutorials.

Implementation handbook (white papers) in Chinese/English

Video tutorial: Step-by-Step Implementation Guide: SAP Document and Reporting Compliance (DRC) Cash Flow Statements

Next Steps

Customers currently using the legacy Cash Flow Statement solution should begin evaluating their migration timeline. Review SAP Note 3700711 - Replacement of Existing 'Cash Flow Statements - China' App with 'China Cash Flow Statements' Report in Document and Reporting Compliance for the latest information on the transition roadmap and consult the implementation handbook for technical guidance.

As with any significant system change, adequate planning and testing are essential. The investment in migration will position your organization to benefit from enhanced performance, improved data consistency, and access to advanced reporting capabilities that support evolving business needs.

For implementation support, please refer to the SAP Help Portal or contact your SAP representative.

 

FAQ:

Q1: What is SAP DRC?

A: SAP Document and Reporting Compliance is a unique and competitive statutory reporting framework that helps you manage your regulatory requirements across the globe. Statutory reporting, a component of SAP Document and Reporting Compliance, is a global solution that enables timely creation, generation, and submission of statutory reports to authorities in the correct format. It ensures compliance with complex and dynamic legal requirements. For more details, refer to SAP Help Portal for Statutory Reporting.

Q2: For S/4HANA Public Cloud customers, does the new DRC Cash Flow Statement require activation of new scope items?

A: Yes.

  • With 1J2, you can generate and download the China Cash Flow Statement in both PDF and TXT(CSV) format.
  • With additional 5XU, you can preview and edit (change the figures and regenerate files with full audit trail), perform analytics, trace back to source documents, and extend the existing report using design time.

Customers need to contact your SAP Account Executive if they have any question related to licenses.

Q3: After the legacy solution is retired, how can customers use the new solution to view historical Cash Flow data?

A: After you activate and configure the DRC cashflow report, you can run the Data Migration Tool (for Public Cloud and OP, or transaction code FIRU_CF_OFF_ACC) to display historical data in the new DRC cashflow report.

Note:

  1. We recommend that you do the solution switch and run the migration program during year-end financial closing or at the beginning of the year.
  2. If you purchased 5XU, you can use the drill-down tool in the analytics view for troubleshooting. If the new solution is configured correctly, the data in the new and legacy solutions should be consistent (legacy solutions use similar offsetting account determination configuration and calculation logic).
  3. This tool only performs data migration. It can't resolve the effort required for customers to implement the new DRC cashflow report.

Q4: What existing legal reports have been replaced by SAP DRC statutory reports so far?

A: SAP Note 2480067 - Replacement of Existing Legal Reports with DRC reports provides information about the legacy ABAP legal reports that statutory reports provided with the SAP Document and Reporting Compliance framework are succeeding.

entry.title: When Synchronous OData Failed for bulk data processing: How I Solved Problem Using OData V4 Async :
entry.content:
The Business Context:

  • BlueYonder sends planning and operational data
  • The data volume is large
  • Data needs to be posted into SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud
  • SAP exposes this functionality via OData V2 and V4 APIs

The expectation was simple: “Send data from BlueYonder and create/update records in S/4HANA.” But in reality, it was not that easy.

Introduction:

In real projects, integrations look simple on paper but behave very differently in production.

This blog is about a real-time problem I faced while integrating BlueYonder planning data into SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud using OData V2 Inbound.

The solution was understanding how OData V4 async processing works and using one small but powerful HTTP header.

In this blog, I will explain:

  • What problem I faced
  • Why synchronous processing failed
  • How I implemented async OData V4 processing with API proxy(through URL).
  • What changed after enabling async

 

The Actual Problem

Initially, I implemented the integration in a synchronous way.

What Happened

  • CPI sent data to S/4HANA public cloud using standard OData V2 synchronous Api
  • The backend took time to process the payload
  • CPI waited for the response
  • Requests started failing due to:
    • Timeouts
    • Long-running backend processing
    • Unstable behaviour during peak loads

The Key Issue

OData V2 APIs are synchronous by default. This means:

  • CPI waits until S/4HANA public cloud finishes posting
  • Large payloads increase risk
  • One slow request can block the entire flow This was not acceptable for a production-grade integration.
  • Chunking the payload using General splitter and streaming could increase the number of API Calls and may increase the load on Public cloud.

Design:

While analysing SAP documentation, I came across the asynchronous processing capability of OData V4. SAP provides a standard way to tell the backend: “Accept the request, process it in the background, and do not keep the connection open.”
This is done using the HTTP header: Prefer = respond-async

Step-by-Step Implementation :

Step 1: Receiving HighRadiusData in CPI

  • BlueYonder sends data in Flatten File.
  • CPI receives the data and prepares it for S/4HANA public cloud.

Data structure is aligned to OData V4 expectations At this point, the flow is still normal.

 

 

 

  • *Here I had implemented the Retry mechanism using JMS Queues which is the best practice for the SAP integrations ,If you like you implement it do check out my previous Blog that I had posted in the SAP Community.
    Blog URL – {https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/handling-connectivity-and-recoverable-errors-in-sap-cpi-with-jms-queues/ba-p/14137974}

Step 2: Setting the Async Header (Most Important Step)

In CPI, I used a Content Modifier to add HTTP headers.

Header : Prefer Header Prefer = respond-async This header completely changes how the request is handled.

 

What Happens After Adding respond-async

Once this header is added:

  • CPI sends the request
  • S/4HANA immediately responds with HTTP 202 – Accepted
  • Backend processing continues asynchronously
  • CPI does not wait for the final processing result This single header removed timeout risks completely.

Step 3: Posting Data via API Proxy

* Here, I had used OAuth 2.0 Authentication between Integration suite to API Proxy and between API Proxy and S/4 Hana public cloud I had used Certificate Based Authentication.

 

  • CPI sends the request to an API Proxy
  • The proxy forwards the call to the OData V4 API from SAP Business Accelerator Hub
  • The proxy is created using the URL-based option

Note : OData V4 services do not appear in the API Provider’s service catalog in SAP API Management, so discovery isn’t possible — and that’s why I go with the URL method, so if you want to create proxy for OData V4 public cloud the correct approach is just go with the URL Option don’t fall for this pitfall.

If you want to leverage more on how to create API Proxy with URL option follow SAP Official Documentation.
Official documentation: {https://help.sap.com/docs/sap-api-management/sap-api-management/create-api-proxy-by-providing-direct-target-endpoint-url}

From CPI’s perspective:

  • The request is completed successfully

No long-running connection is maintained

  • What Changed After This Implementation

    Before Async

    • Timeouts during high data volume
    • Unstable integration behaviour
    • Risk of message failures

    After Async

    • Immediate response to CPI
    • Backend processing happens safely
    • No timeout issues
    • Integration became stable and predictable

    This was a real production-grade improvement, not just a theoretical change, I hope this helps you folks that don’t fall for this pitfall in your production.

entry.title: Timeout in RAP Action
entry.content:
I have a Rap app with an action in a table that is partially executed on up to 100,000 items. Processing then takes place in the backend for each location. Since this process takes a certain amount of time per item, it can take several hours for the entire logic to be completed. As a result, the process aborts in the frontend with a timeout.

I solved the problem by running the processing in an RFC module in the background. The customer does not want to extend the session length that causes the timeout. My question now is whether there are other alternatives to solve this problem?

entry.title: Exception aggregation steps and #NOP together (ABAP RAP Analytical Query)
entry.content:
Hi,

I am trying to define an analytical query in ABAP RAP and I am struggling to have #NOP operation and exception aggregation steps working together in one query.

For simplicity, let's say I have a cube with the following properties: Document, Document Item, Sales and Price. With this simple model, I can define aggregation #SUM for Sales and aggregation #NOP for Price. Let's say I have the following data in my cube:\

Document A, 10, 20, 10
Document A, 20, 40, 10
Document B, 10, 100, 20
Document B, 20, 200, 25

When I ask to get aggregated records by Document and show measures Sales and Price, I get the following two records:

Document A, 60, 10
Document B, 300, <null>

This is all great and what I exactly want to see. The sales get aggregated and Price shows a value only if all items have the same price. Otherwise it does not show the value.

The problem is that my data model is more complex. Let's say each document can be tagged with one or more tags. My cube also has additional attribute Tag. I want provide flexibility to users to have aggregation per tag. Let's say I have the following data in my cube:

Tag One, Document A, 10, 20, 10
Tag One, Document A, 20, 40, 20
Tag Two, Document A, 10, 20, 10
Tag Two, Document A, 20, 40, 20

I have one document, with different prices on each item and the document is associated with two tags. When I ask to get aggregated records by Document and show measures Sales and Price, I get the following record:

Document A, 120, <null>

The aggregation for Price works fine (it shows null as there are different prices for document items). But the aggregation for Sales is double counting as the document has two tags. This is clearly wrong. To fix this problem, I introduced the exception aggregation steps for measure Sales.

@AnalyticsDetails.exceptionAggregationSteps: [ { exceptionAggregationBehavior: #SUM,
exceptionAggregationElements: [ 'Document' ] } ]
Sales

When I ask to get the aggregate records by Document and show measures Sales and Price, I get the following record:

Document A, 60, 10

The aggregation for Sales works again and it does not double count Sales anymore. But the aggregation #NOP stopped working and it shows the value from the first item. I would like to avoid this. The aggregatiobn #NOP is not supported for exceptionAggregationBehavior.

Is there a way how to make #NOP and exception aggregation steps work together? Or is there another way how to model this type of the scenario where the same document can be present multiple times and the summation should prevent any double counting?

The best workaround I could come up with is that I defined two new measures MinPrice and MaxPrice with the corresponding aggregations #MIN and #MAN. Then I defined a new measure with the following formula.

@Aggregation.default: #FORMULA
@EndUserText.label: 'Test'
@AnalyticsDetails.query:{ decimals: 2}
case when $projection.minprice = $projection.maxprice then get_numeric_value(MinPrice) else 0 end as Test,

This almost works as desired. If minimal and maximal prices are the same, it shows the price. Otherwise it shows 0. Here I would prefer null instead of 0. But there are other things that I do not like about this workaround. The hard coded decimals are not nice. And it means that for each measure like Price, I need to define additional 2 measures Min and Max. I have more than 10 fields like this so it quickly gets out of hand. The last concern is about performance of this workaround.

Any suggestions how to achieve this requirement?

Thanks

entry.title: Automating Purchase Requisition Rejection - ME54N and Fiori My Inbox
entry.content:
Background

SAP Note 2012650 – “BAPI_REQUISITION_RELEASE is not possible to reject PR” clearly states that Purchase Requisitions cannot be rejected using BAPI_REQUISITION_RELEASE and that rejection must be performed manually via transaction ME54N.

There are scenarios where manual rejection is not feasible or creates significant operational overhead. In my case, I needed a way to trigger Purchase Requisition rejection programmatically while staying as close as possible to standard behavior.

This requirement led me to search for an alternative approach.

While ME54N is the classic transaction for rejecting PRs, many users also perform approval and rejection activities through Fiori My Inbox. These two entry points do not rely on BAPI_REQUISITION_RELEASE for rejection.

During my analysis, I noticed that the class CL_MM_PUR_PR_APPR_ACTION_BADI plays a central role in handling approval and rejection actions triggered from Fiori. This class became the starting point for my solution.

During my research, I came across a well-written blog describing another approach for Purchase Requisition rejection. Unfortunately, I could not make that solution work in my system, so I explored a different path which resulted in the approach presented here.

Approach

The solution consists of two main steps:

  1. Trigger rejection logic using CL_MM_PUR_PR_APPR_ACTION_BADI

    • This ensures that the approval work item is removed from Fiori My Inbox.

    • The PR is treated consistently with the standard workflow behavior.

  2. Call ME_UPDATE_REQUISITION using classic techniques

    • After the inbox item is cleared, the Purchase Requisition is updated using standard update logic.

    • This mimics the behavior executed when rejection is performed via ME54N.

Important Notes

  • This is not necessarily the best or only approach, but rather one possible alternative when no standard BAPI exists.

  • The provided development is intended as a template only. You can revise and adapt the example class according to your own requirements.
  • It represents a custom automation workaround.

Example Implementation

CLASS zmm_cl_utils_pr DEFINITION
  PUBLIC
  FINAL
  CREATE PUBLIC .

  PUBLIC SECTION.
    METHODS : pr_reject
      IMPORTING iv_banfn            TYPE banfn OPTIONAL
      RETURNING VALUE(RV_completed) TYPE abap_bool
      RAISING   cx_mm_pur_requisition.
  PROTECTED SECTION.
  PRIVATE SECTION.
    METHODS get_release_code
      IMPORTING
        iv_banfn              TYPE banfn
      RETURNING
        VALUE(rv_releasecode) TYPE frgab.
    METHODS set_my_inbox_status_rejected
      IMPORTING iv_banfn TYPE banfn
      RAISING   cx_mm_pur_requisition.
    METHODS set_pr_status_rejected
      IMPORTING iv_banfn            TYPE banfn
      RETURNING VALUE(RV_completed) TYPE abap_bool
      RAISING   cx_mm_pur_requisition.
ENDCLASS.


CLASS zmm_cl_utils_pr IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD pr_reject.
    set_my_inbox_status_rejected( iv_banfn = iv_banfn ).
    rv_completed = set_pr_status_rejected( iv_banfn = iv_banfn ).
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD get_release_code.
    DATA lv_fname TYPE fieldname.

    SELECT SINGLE frgzu, frgst, frggr
      FROM eban
      WHERE banfn = _banfn
      INTO (ls_eban).
    IF sy-subrc <> 0.
      RETURN.
    ENDIF.
    lv_fname = |T16FS~FRGC{ strlen( ls_eban-frgzu ) + 1 }|.
    SELECT SINGLE (lv_fname) FROM t16fs
      WHERE frggr = _eban-frggr
        AND frgsx = _eban-frgst
      INTO @rv_releasecode.
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD set_my_inbox_status_rejected.
    CONSTANTS lc_gname_eban TYPE eqegraname VALUE 'EBAN'.
    DATA lv_wiid          TYPE sww_wiid.
    DATA lv_note          TYPE string.
    DATA lv_save_note     TYPE boolean.
    DATA lv_source_appl   TYPE swc_elem.
    DATA lv_garg          TYPE eqegraarg.
    DATA ls_object        TYPE swr_obj_2.
    DATA lt_enq           TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF seqg3.
    DATA lr_pur_wf_object TYPE REF TO cl_mm_pur_wf_object.

    DATA(lv_like) = |%{ iv_banfn }%|.

    SELECT wi_id, wi_stat FROM swwwihead
      WHERE wi_text LIKE _like
        AND wi_stat    = 'READY'
      ORDER BY wi_cd DESCENDING,
               wi_ct DESCENDING
      INTO TABLE (lt_tab)
      UP TO 1 ROWS.
    IF sy-subrc <> 0.
      RAISE EXCEPTION NEW cx_mm_pur_requisition( textid = VALUE #( msgid = 'SWF_RUN'
                                                                   msgno = '003' ) ).
    ENDIF.

    lv_wiid = lt_tab[ 1 ]-wi_id.
    " -->Get the Purchase Requisition Number, Item from the WorkItem
    CALL FUNCTION 'SAP_WAPI_GET_OBJECTS'
      EXPORTING workitem_id      = lv_wiid
      IMPORTING leading_object_2 = ls_object.
    DATA(lv_pr_num)  = ls_object-instid+0(10).
    DATA(lv_pr_item) = ls_object-instid+10(5).
    IF lv_pr_item IS NOT INITIAL.
      lr_pur_wf_object ?= NEW cl_mm_pur_wf_object_pr_item( iv_object_id = CONV #( ls_object-instid ) ).
    ELSE.
      IF lv_pr_num IS NOT INITIAL.
        lr_pur_wf_object ?= NEW cl_mm_pur_wf_object_pr( iv_object_id = CONV #( ls_object-instid ) ).
      ENDIF.
    ENDIF.

    IF lv_pr_item IS NOT INITIAL.
      " Item Level Release
      SELECT SINGLE PurReqnOrigin,CreatedByUser
        FROM I_PurchaseRequisitionItemAPI01
        INTO (ls_origin)
        WHERE PurchaseRequisition     = _pr_num
          AND PurchaseRequisitionItem = _pr_item.
    ELSE.
      " Overall Release
      SELECT PurReqnOrigin,CreatedByUser
        UP TO 1 ROWS
        FROM I_PurchaseRequisitionItemAPI01
        INTO _origin
        WHERE PurchaseRequisition = _pr_num
        " In case if different users have created different items of PR
        " then the Requester would be the First item user.
        ORDER BY PurchaseRequisitionItem.
      ENDSELECT.
    ENDIF.
    " -->Get Decision
    DATA(lv_decision) = CONV swc_elem( if_mm_pur_workflow_c=>gc_decision_0002 ). " REJECTED
    " -->Check if there is a lock on the PR.only in case of overall release.
    IF lv_pr_item IS INITIAL.
      lv_garg = |{ sy-mandt }{ lv_pr_num }*|.
      CALL FUNCTION 'ENQUEUE_READ'
        EXPORTING  gname                 = lc_gname_eban
                   garg                  = lv_garg
                   guname                = ''
        TABLES     enq                   = lt_enq
        EXCEPTIONS communication_failure = 1
                   system_failure        = 2
                   OTHERS                = 3.
      IF sy-subrc = 0.
        IF lt_enq IS NOT INITIAL.
          RAISE EXCEPTION NEW cx_mm_pur_requisition( textid = VALUE #( msgid = 'MMPUR_PR_WFL_COMMON'
                                                                       msgno = '010' ) ).
        ENDIF.
      ELSE.
        RAISE EXCEPTION NEW cx_mm_pur_requisition( textid = VALUE #( msgid = sy-msgid
                                                                     msgno = sy-msgno
                                                                     attr1 = sy-msgv1
                                                                     attr2 = sy-msgv2
                                                                     attr3 = sy-msgv3
                                                                     attr4 = sy-msgv4 ) ).
      ENDIF.
    ENDIF.

    IF lr_pur_wf_object IS BOUND.
      lr_pur_wf_object->set_workitem_executed( EXPORTING iv_wiid        = lv_wiid
                                                         iv_decision    = lv_decision
                                                         iv_note        = lv_note
                                                         iv_save_note   = lv_save_note
                                                         iv_source_appl = lv_source_appl
                                               IMPORTING es_return      = DATA(ls_return) ).
      IF ls_return IS NOT INITIAL.
        RAISE EXCEPTION NEW cx_mm_pur_requisition( textid = VALUE #( msgid = ls_return-id
                                                                     msgno = ls_return-number
                                                                     attr1 = ls_return-message_v1
                                                                     attr2 = ls_return-message_v2
                                                                     attr3 = ls_return-message_v3
                                                                     attr4 = ls_return-message_v4 ) ).
      ENDIF.
      DATA(lo_pr_approval) = cl_mm_pur_approval=>get_instance( ).
      lo_pr_approval->gs_exe_src-execution_source = if_mm_pur_workflow_c=>gc_execution_source_inb.
      lo_pr_approval->gs_exe_src-scenario_type    = ls_object-catid.
    ENDIF.
    CALL FUNCTION 'BAPI_TRANSACTION_COMMIT'.
    WAIT UP TO '0.1' SECONDS.
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD set_pr_status_rejected.
    DATA t_xeban     TYPE mereq_t_ueban.
    DATA s_xeban     TYPE ueban.
    DATA t_xebkn     TYPE mereq_t_uebkn.
    " TODO: variable is assigned but never used (ABAP cleaner)
    DATA t_container TYPE swrtcont.

    SELECT * FROM eban
      INTO CORRESPONDING FIELDS OF TABLE @t_xeban
      WHERE banfn = _banfn
      ORDER BY PRIMARY KEY.
    CHECK t_xeban IS NOT INITIAL.

    SELECT * FROM ebkn
      INTO CORRESPONDING FIELDS OF TABLE @t_xebkn
      WHERE banfn = _banfn.

    DATA(t_yeban) = t_xeban.
    DATA(t_yebkn) = t_xebkn.

    READ TABLE t_xeban INTO s_xeban INDEX 1.
    s_xeban-kz    = 'U'.
    s_xeban-banpr = '08'.
    MODIFY t_xeban FROM s_xeban INDEX 1.
    CALL FUNCTION 'ME_UPDATE_REQUISITION'
      TABLES xeban = t_xeban
             xebkn = t_xebkn
             yeban = t_yeban
             yebkn = t_yebkn.

    CALL FUNCTION 'ME_UPDATE_REQUISITION_CHNGDOC'
      TABLES xeban = t_xeban
             xebkn = t_xebkn
             yeban = t_yeban
             yebkn = t_yebkn.

    t_container = VALUE #( LET relcode = get_release_code( iv_banfn ) IN
                            FOR wa IN t_xeban
                            ( element = 'PURCHASINGDOCUMENT' value = wa-bnfpo )
                            ( element = 'PURCHASINGITEM'     value = '00010' )
                            ( element = 'RELEASEGROUP'       value = get_release_code( iv_banfn ) ) ).
    CALL FUNCTION 'BAPI_TRANSACTION_COMMIT'.

    DO 100 TIMES.
      WAIT UP TO '0.1' SECONDS.
      SELECT SINGLE  FROM eban
        WHERE banfn = _banfn
          AND banpr = '08' " rejected
        INTO (lv_check).
      IF sy-subrc = 0.
        rv_completed = abap_true.
      ENDIF.
    ENDDO.
    IF lv_check = abap_false.
      RAISE EXCEPTION NEW cx_mm_pur_requisition( textid = VALUE #( msgid = '00'
                                                                   msgno = '001'
                                                                   attr1 = 'Reject could not completed' ) ).
    ENDIF.
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

Conclusion

Although SAP requires manual rejection of Purchase Requisitions, practical project needs sometimes demand automation.

By leveraging the same building blocks used by Fiori My Inbox and ME54N, it is possible to implement a controlled and consistent workaround for programmatic PR rejection.

This approach does not replace SAP standard behavior, but it provides a pragmatic option when no standard API exists.

entry.title: Build Work Zone Returning 401
entry.content:
I have deployed an app on SAP BTP. The app has a service worker as it is a PWA and served through Build Work Zone Managed App Router. Everything works fine until the authentication cookie expires. Once the cookie expires, the app triggers reloading the index.html page, detects that the user is not logged in anymore and takes us to Build Work Zone login screen. In the authentication process, https://*.ondemand.com/login/callback?authType=ias&code
always gets 401 . If I remove the service worker , it works . Even if the service worker does not intercept any request, just it's presence is breaking the auth flow. 
Is there anyone who can help me figure out what's wrong here. Thanks for your time and suggestion.

entry.title: Setting Up Document Grounding with Cloud Integration Automation service: Making Joule context-aware
entry.content:
While Document Grounding has been used to make Joule context-aware with specific business data, the latest development is that integrating Document Grounding with Joule is now fully automated by Cloud Integration Automation service.

In this blog you will find how Cloud Integration Automation service completely automates the integration of Document Grounding with Joule and SharePoint as the data source, hence making Joule context aware.

For more information regarding Document Grounding refer the blogpost : Joule – Getting Started with Document Grounding

Setting up Document Grounding using Cloud Integration Automation service

To set up Document Grounding, you would need to configure many steps as explained in Blog, however, lets now discover how Cloud Integration Automation service can simplify the whole process.

Prerequisites:

  1. Joule instance should be available in one of the SAP BTP subaccounts
  2. You have prepared SharePoint as mentioned in this Blog step “6. Prepare SharePoint Integration “.

Note: You will use SharePoint details later in the Cloud Integration Automation service workflow

Step 1: Subscribe Cloud Integration Automation service via BTP service marketplace. More details can be found : What is Cloud Integration Automation Service? | SAP Help Portal.

Step 2: Once subscribed, launch Cloud Integration Automation service application and click on “Plan for Integration”

Image 1

Step 3: Search for “Document Grounding” scenario

Image 2

You can view scenario related details such as “Description”, “Scenario Entities”, “Scenario Tasks”.

Image 3

Step 4: Click on “Select Systems”.

Image 4

Step 5: Provide system details and “Generate Workflow”

  1. Provide SAP BTP system details

Image 5

    2.  Click on Generate workflow ->Background Execution

Image 6

Note: In the above step, Cloud Integration Automation service will generate a fully automated workflow, and users just need to provide few information to complete the integration.

Click on Next step

Select SharePoint as the data source as highlighted in the below screen shot.

Image 8

Click on Next Step

Image 9

Note: In this step Cloud Integration Automation service will create Share point destination, hence you will use share point information such as Share Point client ID, Share Point Client Secrete, SharePoint Tenant ID, Site URL from the Share Point application that you are using as the data source. You can fetch SharePoint details by referring to the screen shots.

Client ID:

Image 10

Client Secrete:

Image 11

Tenant ID:

Image 12

SharePoint URL:

Image 13

Once you provide all the task parameters click on Next step

Image 14

Click on Confirm

Image 15

Image 16

Step 7: Navigate to Scenario Monitoring to check the workflow status.

Image 17

Here you can observe that all the tasks that are needed for this integration are listed and implemented sequentially.

 Task list:

Task Name

Task Status


Assign Entitlement for Document Grounding Service

Automated

Create Grounding Service Instance with a Service Key

Automated

Create Cloud Identity Service Instance with a Service Key

Automated

Create Destination Service Instance with a Service Key

Automated

Create Destination in BTP for Existing SharePoint

Automated

Create Destination in BTP for Existing SAP Document Management Service

Automated

Create Pipeline for SharePoint

Automated

Create Pipeline for SAP Document Management Service

Automated

 

Upon implementation of all the tasks, Document Grounding is fully integrated with Joule and is context aware with SharePoint as the data source.

Step 8: Go to BTP subaccount to check the following:

1. Instances (groundigcli, grounding Des, identityService) are created along with their service keys

2. Document Grounding destination (docu-grounding-dest-sh) is created

Image 19

Step 9: Launch Joule and ask Joule questions.  You can now get context-aware answers and with links to its document sources.

Image 20

 

Useful Links

 What is Cloud Integration Automation Service? | SAP Help Portal

Joule provisioning with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private using Cloud Integration Automation service

entry.title: Help With Transaction Notification
entry.content:
Hi All,

I have created two TN's on sales order which is working fine.

Working TN:


---- Prevent addition/ update of Sales Order if DocDueDate or ShipDate is Weekend or Holiday----

/* ROW LEVEL CHECK (ShipDate) */
IF EXISTS (
SELECT 1
FROM RDR1 T1
WHERE T1.DocEntry = @List_of_cols_val_tab_del
AND (
DATENAME(WEEKDAY, T1.ShipDate) IN ('Saturday','Sunday')
OR CAST(T1.ShipDate AS DATE) IN (SELECT CAST(StrDate AS DATE) FROM HLD1)
)
)
BEGIN
SET @error = 1717
SET @error_message = 'Sales Order line Ship Date cannot fall on a Weekend or Holiday.'
GOTO TN_RESULT
END


/* HEADER LEVEL CHECK (DocDueDate) */
IF EXISTS (
SELECT 1
FROM ORDR T0
WHERE T0.DocEntry = @List_of_cols_val_tab_del
AND (
DATENAME(WEEKDAY, T0.DocDueDate) IN ('Saturday','Sunday')
OR CAST(T0.DocDueDate AS DATE) IN (SELECT CAST(StrDate AS DATE) FROM HLD1)
)

)
BEGIN
SET @error = 1718
SET @error_message = 'Sales Order Due Date cannot fall on a Weekend or Holiday.'
GOTO TN_RESULT
END

This is working fine.

However, When I consolidate these into a single TN, it doesn't work.

Is this system behavior or something missing in the code:

----------------------------

Consolidated TN not working

--------------------------------

IF EXISTS (
    SELECT 1
    FROM ORDR T0
    INNER JOIN RDR1 T1 ON T0.DocEntry = T1.DocEntry
    WHERE T0.DocEntry = @List_of_cols_val_tab_del
      AND (
            -- Weekend/Holiday check for SO Due Date
            DATENAME(weekday, T0.DocDueDate) IN ('Saturday', 'Sunday')
            OR T0.DocDueDate IN (SELECT H.StrDate FROM HLD1 H)

            -- Weekend/Holiday check for Row ShipDate
            OR DATENAME(weekday, T1.ShipDate) IN ('Saturday', 'Sunday')
            OR T1.ShipDate IN (SELECT H.StrDate FROM HLD1 H)
          )
      AND T0.DocDueDate >= CAST(GETDATE() AS date)
)
BEGIN
    DECLARE @daytype NVARCHAR(100)

    SELECT TOP 1
        @daytype = CASE
                       WHEN DATENAME(weekday, T0.DocDueDate) IN ('Saturday','Sunday')
                            THEN 'Weekend (Header DocDueDate)'
                       WHEN DATENAME(weekday, T1.ShipDate) IN ('Saturday','Sunday')
                            THEN 'Weekend (Row ShipDate)'
                       WHEN T0.DocDueDate IN (SELECT StrDate FROM HLD1)
                            THEN 'Holiday (Header DocDueDate)'
                       WHEN T1.ShipDate IN (SELECT StrDate FROM HLD1)
                            THEN 'Holiday (Row ShipDate)'
                   END
    FROM ORDR T0
    INNER JOIN RDR1 T1 ON T0.DocEntry = T1.DocEntry
    WHERE T0.DocEntry = @List_of_cols_val_tab_del
      AND (
            DATENAME(weekday, T0.DocDueDate) IN ('Saturday', 'Sunday')
            OR DATENAME(weekday, T1.ShipDate) IN ('Saturday', 'Sunday')
            OR T0.DocDueDate IN (SELECT StrDate FROM HLD1)
            OR T1.ShipDate IN (SELECT StrDate FROM HLD1)
          )

    SET @error = 1717
    SET @error_message = 'Date cannot be on a ' + @daytype + '!'
    GOTO TN_RESULT
END

 

Can someone please check and let me know what is missing for the consolidated TN to make it work?

Regards,

Joseph

entry.title: Testing UDVs before Copying or Importing
entry.content:
SAP Business One 10.0 FP 2602 continues to expand its support for User-Defined Values (UDVs). In this release, additional objects, including UDOs and UDTs are now supported. For the full list of newly supported objects, refer to the online help.

A key enhancement introduced in this version is the ability to test UDVs before copying or importing them. This is especially valuable when you are working with a large number of UDVs and want to ensure their accuracy before committing any changes.

Testing before Copying:

In the central UDV management window, you'll find a new option in the Copy menu: “Copy After Test.”

Choosing this option allows you to run a test on the selected UDVs before they are copied, ensuring everything is valid and ready for use.

Testing before Importing:

When importing UDVs from a file, a new “Test” button appears above the preview table. 

Clicking this button triggers a test of all UDVs in the import file before the actual import begins.

Once the test completes, the results are displayed in the preview table, giving you the confidence to continue with the import process. For more details on the import workflow, see the online help.

With these enhancements, SAP Business One now offers a more streamlined and reliable process for copying and importing UDVs, helping you maintain accuracy and optimize your workflow.

entry.title: Re: Request for Standard Process Documentation: Installme...
entry.content:
Good day @O25MBA110462 

Thank you for your question.

You can use the following references to support your learning process

  • Manage Automatic Payments app. This app explain core run logic and due date selection behaviour in automatic payment runs.
  • Schedule Automatic Payments and Postings. A key point you should note here is, the run uses the Posting Date of the next payment run to evaluate whether items are due and should be selected.
  • Manage Automatic Payment reference. Provides you information on scope of proposal and payment execution.

For the installment payment terms in F110 the standard process flow is:

  • You define your main payment term with installment logic for example 30 40 30
  • When you post invoice, the system creates an open item due information which is based on your logic
  • In Manage Automatic Payments, payable lines that are due on the run date rules are selected by the system.
  • Installments are then paid according to each due date of the installment.

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Announcement: Register to the new CEI project for SAP Integration Suite in 2026
entry.content:
Like in the previous years, we will run a Global Customer Engagement Initiative (CEI) for SAP Integration Suite also in 2026.

Last year, we had 180+ registered customers and partners from over 20 countries worldwide. We ran multiple feedback sessions covering various capabilities and strategic investment topics of SAP Integration Suite such as "SAP Process Orchestration move" (ABAP proxy generation, mass migration, migration assessment, migration agent, etc.), AI & gen AI in the context of SAP Integration Suite in general and for B2B in particular, monitoring and operations, new adapters and adapter enhancements, reusable APIs and governance of API key access, event driven architecture, etc.

In 2026, we will continue collaborating with our customers and partners to gather feedback on product improvements, enhancements and new developments to validate the planned enhancements at an early stage, and ensuring that what we build will meet your business needs.

This will include open discussion about our current product backlog from the roadmap explorer as well as addressing pain points and topics that we collected in last year's CEI project such as more on Edge Integration Cell, AI, SAP PI/PO to SAP Integration Suite migration, Event Driven Architecture, ISA-M, best practices and guidelines, developer experience, etc. 

If you like to join the upcoming SAP Integration Suite influence project for providing feedback and shaping the SAP Integration Suite product, please register here (link leads to the customer influence page).

Registration is open from 9th of February 2026 until 13th of March 2026.

Once, the registration closes, we will invite you for an initial call where we go through the scope and the prerequisites of the feedback project. This will be around end of March/ begin of April so that we are able to start the project latest in April this year.

We will run monthly feedback sessions through the end of the year and hold a closing call to summarize our learnings, review the feedback collected, and discuss next steps.

Register today to ensure you do not miss this opportunity to influence and contribute to shaping SAP Integration Suite to meet your business needs. 

We look forward to your active participation and valuable feedback.

entry.title: Re: On-prem infrastructure layout for SAP B1
entry.content:
Hi,

Although production is a very small and simple aspect of our business, we do have a lot of automation, and we have used a physical on site server setup for many years now. The main thing that I know, is that with B1 it is all about the database.

So there is one do in my experience of about 21 years with B1: use a physically separate dedicated server setup for the B1 database. There are three reasons for that:

  1. Shortest possible hardware route to the database for all dependent systems (inc. clients), and dedicated resources for the database, ensuring best possible performance.
  2. When troubleshooting performance issues, ruling out the database setup and hardware is quick and easy. Especially if your RAM and storage are physically dedicated. You avoid surprises by a virtual environment having been setup incorrectly, or some other server or software (accidentally) hogging all resources or filling up your storage due to an error loop, or something like that.
  3. Troubleshooting peripheral systems of B1 is, in my experience fastest / easiest by booting a server or service. When you have to boot the database server, everything stands still, so you don't want to have to do that just because, say, the DI Server is on the fritz.

Regards,

Johan

entry.title: Re: SAP Cloud Integration/CPI: How to Return Custom Error Messages to Sender - available options
entry.content: @atuld For sync interfaces we go for END MESSAGE and Custom Header. Regarding the custom header in error cases, I favor setting it always to the label FAILED and add a custom header with the error code ("BasicAuthNotAllowed" in the example above). Like this, it makes it easier for operations to search for any kind of sync errors by filtering custom status to FAILED. Otherwise, they would need to know which error occurred in order to filter for it... They can still filter for the custom header if the like to.

entry.title: Everything you need to know about || Events in SAP Cloud Integration
entry.content:
Events act as markers within your iFlow, defining where processes start, end, handle errors, or respond to time-based triggers. Each has precise behavior and restrictions.

Start Message: Starting point of the main Integration Process
Initiates the flow by receiving messages from different senders. It connects the sender adapter to the integration flow.

End Message: Ending point of the main Integration Process
Completes the flow and sends data back to the trigger point after processing is done.

Start Event: Starting point of the Local Integration Process
To start a local process within the integration.

End Event: Ending point of the Local Integration Process
To end a local process within the integration

Error Start Event: Starting point of the Exception Subprocess
It catches errors in the main flow or local process to handle exceptions effectively

Error End Event: Ends the exception subprocess when an error occurs, ensuring proper error management, by either ending the subprocess or helping you connect with a Receiver.
If an exception occurs during the processing sequence which has been handled in an Exception Subprocess, the message status displayed in the message processing log is Failed.

Timer: Triggers the iFlow on a scheduled basis, useful for fetching delta records or periodic data from systems when there is no external trigger.
Questions:
❓What happens if the Timer hasn’t finished processing a message before the next scheduled interval?
Ans: If the Timer starts processing a message and it’s not done before the next interval, the Timer skips that next interval.
❓Does Timer support daylight saving?
Ans: Yes
Note: If you want the events to happen at a specific time, with no relation to the specific time of the day during daylight saving, select a time zone that doesn't support daylight saving.
❓Which time standard doesn’t support daylight saving?
Ans: CoordinatedUniversal Time (UTC) is a time standard that isn’t adjusted for daylight saving.
❓Can I use a timer in a Local Integration Process?
Ans: No
❓In case there are 2 Integration Processess in an Integration Flow, can I use a timer in both?
Ans: Yes

Terminate Message: Stops the further processing of the iFlow when a specific condition is met. The flow status in monitoring will reflect as "Completed." For example, you have defined specific values on the payload. If the payload doesn't match those values, the process is terminated.
The message status displayed in the message processing log is Completed, because it has terminated the message successfully.

Questions:
❓Can you use a Terminate Messgae in an Exception Subprocess?
Ans: No

❓By default, what are the events in an Exception Subprocess?

❓Even in case an exception occurs during the processing sequence which has been handled successfully in an Exception Subprocess, you want the message status to be displayed as Failed , then what do you use?
Ans: 1. Use an Error End event. OR
2. Use an Escalation End Event (sets a message status to Escalated in that case).

Escalation End Event: In case the message qualifies one of these categories,
Escalation Event                      : Routing condition has not been met.
Receiver not reachable         : Receiver could not be reached, because it is temporarily down or not accessible (for example, due to overload or maintenance work).
Receiver not found                 : Receiver could not be found because the URL points to a non-existent resource (for example, HTTP 404 error).
Not authenticated to invoke receiver : Receiver could not be called because authentication has failed (for example, HTTP 401 error).
Not authorized to invoke receiver       : Receiver could not be called because of insufficient permissions (for example, HTTP 403 error).
Receiver tries to redirect                        : Receiver could not be reached (HTTP 302 error).
Internal server error in receiver            : Internal server error occurred in the receiver system (for example, HTTP 500 error).
Others – not further qualified               : Escalation category has not been further qualified.
💡If Escalation End Event is in main Integration Process, then Escalated
💡If Integraion Process is calling local integration process, and Escalation End Event is in local integration process, then main flow message will be Completed
💡In case of Synchronous Exchange Pattern, the message will be marked as Escalated
💡In case of Asynchronous Exchange Pattern, no exception will be raised, and the message status will be Escalated
💡If used within an Exception Subprocess, the message processing stops, and the message status will be Escalated. This is regardless of whether the exception subprocess is used in the main Integration Process or a Local Integration Process

 

A quick summary as generated by AI:

 
Event Type
Role / Description
Typical Usage
Message Status
Start Message
Entry point for the main iFlow, connected to sender adapter
Main process begins when a message arrives
Processing/Further Steps
End Message
Exit point for main iFlow, sends processed data to receiver
Final output after integration logic
Completed/Sent
Start Event
Entry for local process within an iFlow
Starts sub-processes, not connected to sender adapter
Processing
End Event
End for local integration process
Concludes local sub-process
Completed
Error Start Event
Entry for exception subprocess (error handling)
Catches errors in main/local process for custom handling
Failed/Escalated
Error End Event
End of exception subprocess, status ‘Failed’
Concludes error handling, updates log
Failed
Timer
Triggers iFlow based on schedule, not on message arrival
Periodic data fetch, no external trigger
Processing/Skipped
Terminate Message
Immediately stops processing upon meeting condition, status ‘Completed’
Conditional logic (e.g., terminate if payload invalid)
Completed
Escalation End Event
Marks message as ‘Escalated’ for exception/routing issues (see list below)
Use for: routing failure, receiver unreachable, auth issues, HTTP errors
Escalated

entry.title: Re: Search Term should be mandatory in CR
entry.content:
Hello @Tahir_Aksoy ,

We normally use the USMD_ACC_FLD_PROP_CUST_DEP_SET to make fields mandatory and/or use USMD_RULE_SERVICE to prevent the workflow from being submitted by letting the user know about mandatory fields as a validation message. Those are the enhancement spots we are using for complex scenarios such as your goal.

It doesn't cover your case, but if you are seeking a general customization point to make a field mandatory, you can achieve that by making the related attribute of the data model "Required Entry Field" under MDGIMG->Classic Mode in SAP MDG->Central Governance->General Settings->Data Modeling->Edit Data Model. There is no configuration point to make a field mandatory per role under MDGIMG.

When you ask the version and date of LLM Claude 4.5 Haiku, the answer from chat will tell you this:
 

# My Information

## Version
**Claude 3.5 Sonnet** (as of my last update in April 2024)

## Training Data
- **Knowledge cutoff**: April 2024
- **Dataset composition**: I was trained on a large corpus of text from the internet, books, academic papers, and other publicly available sources
- **Training approach**: Constitutional AI (CAI) - trained using RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback) with a focus on being helpful, harmless, and honest

## Key Limitations
- I don't have access to real-time information
- I can't browse the internet
- My knowledge has a cutoff date (April 2024)
- I can't access external databases or APIs
- I don't learn or update from our conversations

## What I Can Help With
- Answering questions based on my training data
- Writing, analysis, and creative tasks
- Code and technical problems
- Research and explanations
- Problem-solving and brainstorming

Is this a bug? How can i fix it?

entry.title: Re: SAP BTP-CPI-Package-Domain-Centric-Pattern (PDCP):Solving Package Sprawl at Scale-PART II
entry.content:
@vinaymittal 

Thank you for taking the time to share a detailed and thoughtful perspective. These discussions are essential in enterprise architecture, particularly because approaches that work well for isolated integrations often reveal their limitations when applied at global scale.

Your feedback appears to assess the proposal primarily from a tactical implementation perspective, focused on individual interface simplicity. The intent of the DCRP and PDCP frameworks, however, is to address enterprise platform governance, long-term sustainability, and systemic risk — a different architectural layer. Allow me to clarify that strategic intent:

1. Leadership, Discipline, and Architectural Order

The use of strategic metaphors, including “The Commander”, is not about conflict, but about discipline, accountability, and decision ownership. In mission-critical landscapes processing tens of thousands of messages daily, insufficient governance does not fail gracefully — it compounds operational risk. The framework emphasizes architectural order as a preventive control against uncontrolled landscape entropy.

2. Resilience Through Abstraction (Role of SAP API Management)

The framework does not position SAP API Management as mandatory, but as architecturally justified in scenarios aligned with SAP’s Clean Core strategy and perimeter security principles.

The gateway layer acts as a facade and abstraction boundary, enabling backend evolution, vendor changes, or migrations without impacting consumers. This is not added complexity — it is resilience by design, activated only when scale and exposure demand it.

3. Business Domains Over Vendor-Centric Structures

Vendor-based grouping is often effective at small scale, but it introduces long-term coupling.

PDCP aligns packages with business domains, treating source systems as interchangeable technical components. This mirrors organizational ownership, simplifies impact analysis, and ensures that the architectural structure survives vendor turnover — a recurring reality in enterprise landscapes.

4. Reducing the Administrative Surface Area

Secret vaults solve the storage of credentials, but not governance topology.

From an operational governance standpoint, managing fewer, tiered technical users significantly reduces permission sprawl, audit complexity, and the probability of configuration errors within SAP BTP Cockpit. The objective is not merely fewer credentials, but fewer governance failure points.

5. “Order by Design” vs. Deferred Complexity

In enterprise environments, “starting simple” often leads to unstructured growth, which later becomes costly legacy.

An upfront, domain-centric framework enables rapid onboarding and deployment (measured here at an 18.8× improvement), precisely because governance and routing concerns are already standardized and absorbed by the platform.

“Strategy without tactics is the slowest route to victory.Tactics without strategy is the noise before defeat.” — Sun Tzu

DCRP and PDCP are not about adding complexity; they are about introducing intentional structure so that scale can be achieved without chaos.

The upcoming Part III will focus on concrete implementation details and measurable operational outcomes, which should further clarify how these principles translate into practice.

Best regards,
Ricardo Viana
Enterprise Integration Architect

entry.title: How to Restrict Access to Employee Data Based on Responsibility Period? - SAP SuccessFactors
entry.content:
Time‑Restricted Access to Employee Records: Why “When” and “Where” Matter! 

Rina just became the manager of Dev, who moved to her team on April 1, 2025. On her first day, Rina tries to open Dev’s entire work history to understand his past assignments back to 2019. She’s surprised: she can only see records from April 1, 2025 onward—nothing before. Meanwhile, Amol in Payroll can see Dev’s UK records but not his earlier US ones. Why the difference? Because their access is now restricted by time of responsibility and legal entity responsibility, not just by a broad role like “Manager” or “Payroll Admin.”

This blog explains, in simple terms, how period‑restricted access works in SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central for managers, job relationship roles, and permission‑group (admin) roles—and why it’s a meaningful shift from traditional role‑based access that exposed all historical time slices.

What changed—and why it matters

Historically, role‑based permissioning in many HR systems allowed anyone with a role (e.g., Manager or HR Admin) to view an employee’s entire history of effective‑dated records (job info, personal info, compensation, etc.). That created two problems:

  1. Over‑exposure of history: New managers could see records from periods when they had no responsibility.
  2. Complex compliance scenarios: Admins responsible for specific legal entities sometimes saw records for employees outside those entities.

Period‑restricted access fixes this by overlaying time (and, for admins, legal entity responsibility) on top of role‑based permissions. Result: people see only the records that fall within their responsibility period, and admins see only records for in‑scope legal entities within the dates they were responsible.

Supported HRIS entities include effective‑dated data like Job Information, Job Relationships, Personal Information, Dependent Information, Address Information, and Compensation Information on the latest People Profile (view, edit, and History UI) and EC Quick Actions.
 

Key concepts at a glance

  • Responsibility period (time): The start and end dates during which a manager (or a job‑relationship role like matrix manager, HR manager, or custom manager) is formally associated to the employee. Access is limited to only those dates across the employee’s effective‑dated records.
  • Permission‑group admins (scope): For roles granted via permission groups, access is further limited to the legal entities selected as “responsible” for that role. Within those entities, time rules still apply—you see only the time slices that fall within your responsibility period.
  • Direct associations only: Period‑restricted access covers direct managers and supported job‑relationship types (e.g., HR manager, matrix manager, second/additional/custom manager). It doesn’t auto‑roll up to higher or lower org levels.
  • Multiple time‑based roles: If a user holds multiple time‑bounded roles (say, direct manager and matrix manager at different times), their access becomes the union of those responsibility periods.
  • Not for analytics/API/imports: The restriction applies to People Profile/Quick Actions, not to analytics reports, APIs, or import sources.

Example 1 — New manager can’t see “before my time”

Scenario

  • Employee: Susan
  • Change: International transfer from the US to the UK effective January 1, 2022
  • Managers:
    • Robert (US HR Administrator / prior manager) — responsible until December 31, 2021
    • Paula (UK HR Administrator / new manager) — responsible from January 1, 2022 onward

How the system checks access

  1. It looks at the dates each manager is associated to Susan (their responsibility periods).
  2. It applies these dates across Susan’s effective‑dated Personal Information (and other HRIS entities) to decide what each manager can view/edit/insert.

What each manager can see/do

  • April 1, 2022 – Dec 31, 9999 (current period):

    • Paula: Can access (view/edit).
    • Robert: No access (outside his responsibility period).

Dec 1, 2021 – Mar 31, 2022 (bridge window around the transfer):

  • Robert: Can view/edit and insert until Dec 31, 2021.
  • Paula: Can view/edit and insert from Jan 1, 2022.
  • This reflects the precise hand‑off date where old manager stops editing/inserting records and new manager starts. However, both can view the entire time slice as it's overlapping time of responsibility.

Jan 1, 2021 – Nov 30, 2021 (older history):

  • Robert: Can access (was responsible then).
  • Paula: No access (was not responsible yet).

Key Takeaway: 

Paula, the new manager, cannot see or edit time slices before Jan 1, 2022. Robert, the former manager, cannot see or edit anything after Dec 31, 2021. The system enforces the “only during my watch” rule automatically, time‑slicing every supported HRIS entity.
 

Example 2 — Legal‑entity–based restriction for permission‑group admins

Scenario

  • Employee: Susan
  • Legal Entities:
    • ORG1_US (before transfer)
    • ORG1_UK (after transfer)
  • Payroll Admins (granted via permission groups):
    • John → responsible for ORG1_US (until Dec 31, 2021)
    • Alex → responsible for ORG1_UK (from Jan 1, 2022)

How the system checks access

  1. It evaluates whether the employee’s legal entity on a given time slices in job histories are among the admin’s responsible legal entities configured and selected in the permission role.
  2. Then it overlays time of responsibility to determine which slices the admin can view/edit/insert for the same or other effective-dated HRIS entities.

What each admin can see/do

  • Apr 1, 2022 – Dec 31, 9999 (ORG1_UK):

    • Alex (UK admin): Access granted.
    • John (US admin): No access.

  • Dec 1, 2021 – Mar 31, 2022 (transfer window; LE changes on Jan 1, 2022):

    • Alex: Access and may create new records from Jan 1, 2022 (when Susan joins ORG1_UK).
    • John: Access and may create new records until Dec 31, 2021 (while Susan is still in ORG1_US).

    Jan 1, 2021 – Nov 30, 2021 (ORG1_US):

    • John: Access granted.
    • Alex: No access.

    Key Takeaway: 

    Permission‑group admins are not global by default. Their access is limited to the legal entities they’re responsible for and the dates of that responsibility—mirroring the employee’s effective‑dated records.
     

    How to enable and configure (quick checklist) in 2H 2025 Release

    Prerequisites (Admin Center → Company System and Logo Settings):

    • Turn on Full Profile for the latest People Profile. - This feature requires new/latest people profile enabled to work.
    • Enable Period‑Restricted Access for Managers.
    • Enable Period‑Restricted Access for Job Relationships.
    • Enable Period‑Restricted Access for Permission Groups.
      Then, in Configure Latest People Profile, enable the latest Editing and History UI for EC HRIS entities (except Compensation Information).

    Role‑based permissions (Admin Center → Manage Permission Roles):

    • For Managers: Grant to Managers, target Granted User’s Direct Reports.
    • For Job Relationships: Grant to supported relationship types (HR manager, matrix, additional/custom), target Matrix Reports as applicable.
    • For Permission Groups: Grant to Select Groups; in Manage Period‑Restricted Access, choose the Responsible Legal Entity(ies) for that role. 

    Notes & boundaries

    • If a role is not enabled for period restriction in Company Settings, users in that role are treated as administrators with unrestricted access.
    • Period‑restricted access currently does not apply to analytics, APIs, or import change sources.
    • The feature supports direct managers/relationships only—no automatic roll‑ups.
    • Overlapping records with time of responsibility of multiple managers or admins will be visible by both old and new. However, edit/insert will be restricted based on time of responsibility.

    Summary

    • Privacy by design: People only see what they’re responsible for—and only when they’re responsible.
    • Clean hand‑offs: On the effective date of a transfer or manager change, access shifts automatically—no lingering visibility into past or future slices.
    • Granular compliance: Legal‑entity scoping for admins ensures regional or entity‑specific teams don’t over‑reach.

entry.title: Custom Number Range for Business Partner while doing CVI in a ECC to S/4HANA Conversion
entry.content:
Hello All,

I am working on a CVI activity as part of an ECC to S/4HANA Private Cloud conversion project. In the current ECC system, Customers and Vendors share overlapping number ranges—for example, Customers use 100000–199999 and Vendors use 100000–200000.

The client expects the corresponding Business Partners in S/4HANA to follow new, prefixed number ranges:

  • Customer BPs: 1000100000–1000199999
  • Vendor BPs: 2000100000–2000200000

Essentially, they want to add prefixes of 1000 for customer BPs and 2000 for vendor BPs.

Based on my understanding, during CVI we can either:

  1. Use the same number for the BP as the existing Customer/Vendor, or
  2. Use an internal BP number range.

However, we cannot manipulate BP number assignment to automatically add prefixes or transform the number during CVI synchronization.

I would appreciate a peer review to confirm whether my understanding is correct.

If you want, I can also help you draft a more assertive version, a more formal version, or one that includes SAP notes and technical references.

entry.title: New course: Positioning SAP Order Management Services
entry.content:
We’re excited to introduce a brand-new learning opportunity for sales and pre-sales professionals:

Positioning SAP Order Management Services 

Why SAP Order Management Services?

In today’s digital-first economy, businesses face increasing challenges in managing their order processes. From handling multiple sales channels and meeting rising customer expectations to integrating with legacy systems and reducing errors, order management has never been more complex. SAP Order Management Services (OMS) is engineered to solve exactly these challenges.

What Will You Learn?

This focused course goes beyond product features. It guides you through:

  • The challenges faced by modern businesses in order management: Understand the pain points your customers are experiencing, such as supply chain visibility gaps, inefficiencies in order handling, and the need for real-time inventory management.
  • The end-to-end capabilities of SAP OMS: Discover how SAP OMS covers the full order process lifecycle, from order capture and validation to fulfillment, tracking, and returns, seamlessly integrated across all channels and touchpoints.
  • Business value drivers and tangible benefits: Learn how to articulate the business case, showing customers how SAP OMS improves operational efficiency, elevates the customer experience, reduces costs, boosts revenue, and supports scalability and innovation.
  • Inspiring customer success stories: See how real-world organizations have transformed their order management processes and driven measurable business outcomes with SAP OMS, stories you can share to build trust and credibility with your clients.

Who Should Enroll?

  • Sales consultants working with SAP customers or prospects
  • Pre-sales consultants looking to stand out with specialized knowledge
  • Anyone eager to understand how SAP OMS drives smarter business operations

Learn Today!

Ready to master SAP OMS positioning? Start learning now and take the first step toward equipping yourself with the knowledge and strategies you need to confidently position SAP Order Management Services.

 

We hope you enjoy this course and encourage you to share your valuable feedback with us. Thank you!

entry.title: Re: SAP BTP-CPI-Package-Domain-Centric-Pattern (PDCP):Solving Package Sprawl at Scale-PART II
entry.content:
Thank you for the response. I appreciate the clarification on strategic intent.

However, I'm still looking for concrete answers to the practical concerns raised:

Cost justification: What is the actual TCO comparison? API Management licenses, implementation hours, ongoing maintenance vs. the deployment time savings claimed?

Real-world validation: You mention 18.8× deployment improvement. Can you share:
 Actual before/after metrics from a production landscape?
 Team size and skill level required?
 Timeline for implementation?

Edge cases**: How does this handle:
- Emergency hotfixes requiring single iFlow deployment?
- Different security requirements per vendor (e.g., PCI compliance for payments)?
- Gradual migration from existing sprawl?
- Different quota/Whitelisting policy needs per API Endpoint 

The framework presents interesting concepts, but without implementation evidence and cost-benefit analysis, it's difficult to evaluate whether this is genuinely more sustainable than established patterns like logical grouping with strong naming conventions.

entry.title: Re: How to assign role to communication user in s/4hana p...
entry.content:
Good day @Yuexin_Jin 

Thank you for your question.

That error message in your XML is key. What you are experiencing is an authorisation/start permission issue for the service, not a UI5 coding issue.

The reference below provides guidance:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/2743304

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3462808

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0f69f8fb28ac4bf48d2b57b9637e81fa/a0771f6765f54e1c8193ad8582a32edb.html

From your screenshot, your arrangement has both API_BILLING_DOCUMENT_SRV (OData V2) and api_billingdocument/srvd_a2x (OData v4) but you called /sap/opu/odata/sap/API_BILLING_DOCUMENT_SRV/...  and got start authentication error. That error (see KBA 3462588) could originate from a few factors such as:

  • Your communication arrangement is inconsistent or not fully active
  • You choose wrong authentication path
  • Your communication user credentials are not being sent correctly by the destination
  • API not available in your tenant scope/release combination. Etc.

Please note that, you do not need your BTP trial account to be in the same tenant landscape as s4 cloud to make your intent work. Deploying the app inside the same system will not fix your specific error.

I recommend you:

  1. Verify SAP_COM_O120 is active, and the exact OData endpoint is used from your communication arrangement.
  2. Then verify communication user is not lock and the attached credentials are valid.
  3. Test the endpoint with basic auth in Postman first and,
  4. Ensure your BTP destination sends communication-user credentials and that they point to the same API host shown in your arrangement.

In summary, a separate BTP account is not the core issue you are facing. If the same error persists in Postman and UI5, I suggest you raise an incident with arrangement, endpoint and error.

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris
