entry.title: Joule Is Not a Chatbot: How AI Agents on SAP BTP Help North American Utilities Reduce Risk
entry.content:
Introduction

 In North American utilities, regulatory expectations shape almost every operational decision. Over the years working with U.S. and Canadian utilities, I’ve seen how state and provincial rules influence even the smallest processes — from how quickly meter reads must be validated to how consistently billing exceptions must be resolved.

Regulators typically define:

  • Required timelines for completing meter reads

  • SLA windows for resolving billing exceptions

  • Limits on consecutive estimated bills

  • Documentation standards for every action taken

When these expectations aren’t met, the consequences are real: customer credits, revenue adjustments, additional reporting, and increased regulatory scrutiny. Even with strong CIS and MDM systems, I’ve seen exception queues grow faster than teams can manage. SLA timers often run in the background without real-time visibility, and escalations still depend heavily on someone noticing an issue manually.

This gap between regulatory expectations and operational reality is exactly where AI agents can make a meaningful difference.

Main Content

1. Regulatory Context for North American Utilities

In North American utilities, regulatory expectations shape almost every operational decision. Over the years working with U.S. and Canadian utilities, I’ve seen how state and provincial rules influence even the smallest processes — from how quickly meter reads must be validated to how consistently billing exceptions must be resolved.

Regulators typically define required timelines, SLA windows, limits on estimated bills, and documentation standards. When these expectations aren’t met, utilities face customer credits, revenue adjustments, additional reporting, and increased scrutiny. Exception queues grow quickly, SLA timers run without visibility, and escalations depend on manual intervention.

This is where AI agents can make a meaningful difference.

2. Why Joule Is More Than a Chatbot

During my learning journey with Joule, one misconception stood out immediately: many people assume Joule is “just another chatbot.” But when you look at how it works on SAP BTP, it becomes clear that Joule is fundamentally different.

A chatbot answers questions. A Joule Agent takes action.

From a practitioner’s perspective, what makes Joule valuable is its ability to:

  • Understand business rules and regulatory requirements

  • Monitor operational data continuously

  • Detect exceptions before they breach SLAs

  • Trigger automated workflows

  • Maintain complete audit logs for compliance

In regulated industries like utilities, this shift from conversational assistance to policy-driven automation is significant. It’s not about chatting — it’s about operational discipline.

 

3. Why AI Agents Matter for Exception Management

Exception management has always been one of the most resource-intensive areas in utilities. Across multiple programs, I’ve seen the same patterns repeat:

  • No-read or zero-consumption meters

  • High/low usage variances

  • Billing holds

  • Field-service-dependent issues

  • High-bill complaints

Each of these carries its own SLA clock. When thousands of exceptions accumulate, manual monitoring simply can’t keep up — even with experienced teams.

AI agents help by addressing the exact pain points I’ve seen utilities struggle with:

3.1 Continuous Monitoring

Agents watch exceptions as they occur, not hours or days later.

3.2 Risk-Based Prioritization

They highlight issues that could cause regulatory or revenue impact — something analysts often have to do manually.

3.3 Autonomous Escalation

They trigger workflows or notify supervisors without waiting for human review, reducing the dependency on tribal knowledge.

 

4. How Joule Agents Work on SAP BTP

A Joule Agent is not a standalone tool — it’s an orchestrator that brings multiple BTP services together.

4.1 Data and Event Integration

It listens to events from systems such as:

  • SAP S/4HANA Utilities

  • SAP MDM

  • SAP Event Mesh

  • SAP HANA Cloud

In real operations, these events include new exceptions, updated SLA timers, meter reads, or billing document changes.

4.2 Policy and Rule Interpretation

Regulatory rules are translated into thresholds, categories, and escalation paths — something utilities typically manage through spreadsheets or manual SOPs.

4.3 Workflow Execution

The agent can start SAP Build Process Automation workflows, reassign cases, or notify supervisors.

4.4 Audit Logging

Every action is logged for compliance — a major benefit for regulatory reporting.

 

5. Practical Example: SLA Monitoring for Meter-Reading Exceptions

Here’s a scenario I’ve seen repeatedly: A utility must resolve meter-reading exceptions within 48 hours. These include no-reads, communication failures, and high/low variances.

Without AI Agents

  • Exceptions pile up

  • Analysts manually review queues

  • Supervisors rely on periodic reports

  • SLA timers aren’t monitored continuously

  • Escalations depend on human judgment

This often leads to missed deadlines and inconsistent documentation — especially during peak seasons.

 

Figure 1: SLA monitoring lifecycle for meter-reading exceptions using Joule Agents.

 

With a Joule Agent

Step 1: Monitors exceptions and SLA timers in real time Step 2: Flags high-risk or time-sensitive cases Step 3: Automatically triggers workflows, reassigns cases, and notifies supervisors Step 4: Captures all actions for compliance

Outcome:

  • Fewer SLA breaches

  • Less revenue leakage

  • Stronger compliance

  • Better use of staff time

From a practitioner’s perspective, this is exactly the kind of automation utilities have been trying to achieve for years.

 

6. Reference Architecture

A typical setup includes:

  • SAP S/4HANA Utilities

  • SAP MDM

  • SAP Event Mesh

  • SAP HANA Cloud

  • Joule Agent

  • SAP Build Process Automation

  • SAP Audit Log Service

This architecture can be rolled out gradually and adapted to existing landscapes — something important for utilities with complex legacy environments.

 
Figure 2: Reference architecture for Joule Agent orchestration across SAP BTP services.

 

7. Governance and Compliance

Regulators expect utilities to demonstrate:

  • Proactive monitoring

  • Consistent rule application

  • Clear escalation paths

  • Strong audit evidence

Joule Agents help by applying rules consistently, reducing manual errors, and maintaining detailed logs — all of which strengthen compliance posture.

 

8. Organizational Impact

Utilities that adopt AI agents typically see improvements in:

  • Exception resolution speed

  • SLA compliance

  • Revenue protection

  • Operational efficiency

  • Employee workload balance

Supervisors gain real-time visibility, analysts focus on solving issues instead of chasing timers, and compliance teams get better documentation.

 

Conclusion

Joule is not a chatbot. It’s an AI agent framework designed to help utilities automate compliance, reduce revenue leakage, and manage exceptions proactively. Based on what I’ve seen across multiple utility programs, the shift from reactive exception handling to automated, risk-aware operations is becoming essential — and Joule Agents on SAP BTP provide a practical path to get there.

entry.title: Re: JMS Mastery Series Part 2 - The JMS Revolution
entry.content:
Hi Mateusz,

Great article—really enjoyed it. Reading about SOAP One-Way (Robust) got me thinking: why doesn’t this adapter return the sap_messageprocessinglogid header to the sender?

This isn’t really a question for you, but I find it curious that after executing the entire iFlow, this header isn’t part of the response.

It would be great to have it included for extended traceability.

Thanks for the effort and for sharing this.

Let’s go for part three, sounds promising!

entry.title: Hands-on Tutorial (SAP) Databricks triggering ML in SAP Datasphere
entry.content:
Did you know that SAP Databricks and native Databricks can trigger Machine Learning in SAP Datasphere? Get a Databricks Notebook to trigger SAP's Predictive Analysis Library. No data needs to be moved around.

 

 

SAP Databricks is a Data Science environment within SAP Business Data Cloud. It is primarily intended to access data products from SAP Business Data Cloud for you to build your own custom AI/ML models with its built-in Databricks serverless compute. However, you can also use it to instruct SAP Datasphere to process its data and to create Machine Learning forecasts with SAP HANA Cloud's built-in Machine Learning algorithms. Let's try it out together. 

Note that the data and code /can be downloaded from this repository:
Hands-on Tutorial (SAP) Databricks triggering ML in SAP Datasphere

Table of contents 

  • Use Case
  • Architecture
  • Prerequisites
  • SAP Datasphere Script Server
  • SAP Datasphere Database User
  • Data upload
  • Demand forecast
  • Store Database User Credentials
  • Connection to SAP Datasphere
  • Data Exploration
  • Data Preparation
  • Time series forecasting
  • Using the forecast in SAP Datasphere
  • Summary

 

Use Case

The use case is inspired by a requirement a logistics customer brought up recently. They keep granular data in SAP Datasphere about the items that shipped in the past. One row of data per shipment, about 80 million records. This data should now be used to create a forecast of how many items are expected in the upcoming days. These estimates will feed into their workforce planning, to have the appropriate number of staff at hand.

Well, for implementing this scenario I have no data from the real world of millions of parcels being processed. The next best data that I could find for this hands-on example is a detailed count of vehicles in Zurich, Switzerland. The city has a number of measurement stations across the town that provide an hourly count of how many vehicles drove by. So instead of predicting the number of expected parcels, we will predict the number of vehicles. The concept is technically the same. The detailed data (3 million records in the Zurich data) needs to be explored, prepared, aggregated and then used as basis for a prediction.

 

Architecture

Let's keep things simple for this example, and we will create the forecast with a time-series algorithm. The daily count of vehicles that were counted is used to predict how many vehicles (or parcels) are expected in the next few days. The data is stored in a table in SAP Datasphere. 

SAP Databricks is used as development environment to script the Python code, that instructs SAP HANA Cloud within SAP Datasphere to create that prediction and to save it to a table in SAP Datasphere. Instead of SAP Databricks you can also follow this tutorial with a native instance of Databricks.

You will see in the steps below, how these instructions are created. But to give you the big picture right away: SAP's Python package hana_ml is used in SAP Databricks to create the SQL syntax that is sent to SAP HANA Cloud to trigger its embedded Predictive Analysis Library.

These links might help to find out more about the Predictive Analysis Library. It contains 100+ different algorithms, all implemented by SAP to make best use of SAP HANA Cloud's architecture.

  • Course: Developing Regression Models with the Python Machine Learning Client for SAP HANA
  • Documentation: Python Machine Learning Client for SAP HANA (hana_ml)
  • Documentation: Predictive Analysis Library 

 

Prerequisites

To follow this tutorial hands-on you need a SAP Business Data Cloud environment with SAP Datasphere and SAP Databricks. Some familiarity with SAP Databricks would help, for instance having gone through this tutorial, which can be implemented with the free trial of SAP Business Data Cloud.

 

SAP Datasphere Script Server

SAP Datasphere must have the Script Server enabled, so that the Predictive Analysis Library can be used, please see SAP Note 3216010. The Script Server is only available if your system has at least 3 virtual CPUs. Only the very smallest systems are running with 2 vCPUs. If this should apply to your environment, increasing the memory will get you there.

 

SAP Datasphere Database User

The Python code in SAP Databricks will connect to SAP Datasphere with a Database User. To create this user go in SAP Datasphere to "Space Management", edit your space and in the "Database Users" section create that user. Give it a name and tick the options in the screenshot. These settings ensure that the user can access the data and write predictions to a table.

 

Click the "info" icon on the right and the credentials show up. You will need

  • The Database User name (including the name of the space followed by '#')
  • The Host name
  • The Port (always 443)
  • And the password (the password is only shown once. If you lose it, you need to request a new one)

 

Data upload

To keep things focussed, I have created a CSV file with detailed vehicle counts that you can upload as a single file. In this example, we ignore some important facts that you would need to consider in a real project. For instance the number of measurement stations has been changing over time. We will just take the data as it is, so that we can focus on the end-to-end tutorial without getting lost in the nitty gritty details. After all, the vehicle data is just an example. In reality we should have a clean and full history of all parcels.

Download the historic data and upload the unzipped file in the Data Builder with "Import CSV file". 

 

Deploy the table with the suggested name VEHICLECOUNT. And we have data. Each location might have one or more sensors. For each sensor we have hourly measurements. 

 

Currently the Database User cannot yet access the table though. You just need to put a view on top of the table, which has the option "Expose for Consumption" activated. In the little screencam you see that some records have NULL values for the vehicle count. This is a data quality issue from the raw data provided by the city. The sensors might not have been working at that time. We will ignore that in this tutorial. In the real world you may want to look into this as part of the data preparation, maybe by imputing the missing data..

 

 

Demand forecast  

All code that is described in this tutorial is part of this repository. Just download and import the DBC archive file into SAP Databricks.

 

You now have a project called "Logistics forecast", which contains two notebooks:

  • Store Database User Credentials
  • Logistics forecast with HANA ML

 

Store Database User Credentials

Continue by putting your Database User's credentials in a safe place.  Instead of hardcoding them into each notebook, store them centrally as Databricks secret. Open the notebook "Store Database User Credentials". Enter your own Database User's credentials in the code and run the cells. 

dbuser_address = "yourdbuseraddress"
dbuser_user = "yourdbuser"
dbuser_password = "yourdbuserpassword"

from databricks.sdk import WorkspaceClient
w = WorkspaceClient() 
scope = "sapdatasphere_dbuser"
w.secrets.create_scope(scope)
w.secrets.put_secret(scope,"address",string_value=dbuser_address)
w.secrets.put_secret(scope,"user",string_value=dbuser_user)
w.secrets.put_secret(scope,"password",string_value=dbuser_password)

Each notebook can now retrieve the credentials from that secret. And to the best of my knowledge, SAP Databricks keeps the values so safe, that you cannot even seem them in clear text yourself. You can try to print them for instance, but it will only show "[REDACTED]" 

 

Connection to SAP Datasphere

Now open the notebook "Logistics forecast with HANA ML". All code that we need for the forecasting is already in that file. In the 'Environment'-tab on the right, you see that the Python package hana_ml has been added. Clear the Notebook's output, to be sure that any future output you see, was created by yourself.

 

Run the first cells to retrieve the Database User credentials from the Databricks secret. Use these to establish a connection to SAP Datasphere.

dbuser_address = dbutils.secrets.get(scope="sapdatasphere_dbuser", key="address")
dbuser_user = dbutils.secrets.get(scope="sapdatasphere_dbuser", key="user")
dbuser_password = dbutils.secrets.get(scope="sapdatasphere_dbuser", key="password")

import hana_ml.dataframe as dataframe
conn = dataframe.ConnectionContext(address=dbuser_address,
                                   port=443, 
                                   user=dbuser_user, 
                                   password=dbuser_password, 
                                  )
conn.connection.isconnected()

The isconnected() function should return "True".

  • If you are getting an error that the hana_ml package is missing, go back to the 'Environment'-tab and hit "Apply". This will install the package.
  • If you are getting a Connection failed error "Socket closed by peer", then most likely the Notebook's external IP address hasn't been added yet to SAP Datasphere's allow list. You can see the IP address with this command. 
from requests import get
ip = get('https://api.ipify.org').text
print(f'The public IP address is: {ip}')

Then add this address to the allow list as described in the documentation. Currently (February '26) Databricks has a feature in Preview mode to get a static IP address. You can reach out to your Databricks account manager to request having this feature activated in your environment. In case you don't know that person, feel free to ping me and I will try to make a connection.

 

Data Exploration

Once you are connected, point the hana_ml DataFrame to the Datasphere view you had created earlier. This does not extract any data from SAP Datasphere. The 3 million records remain in place. But you can download rows if you like. Just select 5 rows, to verify the connection does indeed work as expected. Then call a canned report for further data understanding. The details are also calculated within SAP HANA Cloud.

current_schema = conn.get_current_schema().split('#')[0]
data_hdf = conn.table('V_VEHICLECOUNT', schema=current_schema)
data_hdf.head(5).collect()
from hana_ml.visualizers.unified_report import UnifiedReport
UnifiedReport(data_hdf).build().display()

 

Data Preparation

We would like a daily forecast, but our data is more detailed with hourly timestamps. Hence create a new column that contains only the date, cutting off the hourly information. This column is not persisted, it does not change the underlying view. It is calculating the new column on the fly, when accessing the data.

data_hdf = data_hdf.select('*', ('TO_DATE(MEASUREDATETIME)', 'MEASUREDATE'))
data_hdf.head(5).collect()

 

Having the MEASUREDATE column make it easy to summarise the measurements by date.

data_hdf = data_hdf.agg([('sum', 'VEHICLECOUNT', 'VEHICLECOUNT')], group_by='MEASUREDATE')
data_hdf = data_hdf.sort_values('MEASUREDATE')
data_hdf.head(5).collect()

 

All aggregations were done in SAP Datasphere / SAP HANA Cloud. Under the hood the aggregation request was translated by hana_ml into the corresponding SQL syntax, which was sent to SAP HANA Cloud. You can see the SELECT statement that was executed.

data_hdf.select_statement

 

Plot the daily values. The collect() function downloads the aggregated data as Pandas DataFrame. There seems to be a trend that more vehicles are counted. This could be because of increased traffic, or an increased number of sensors. For this tutorial we won't look any deeper into this. But a weekly pattern seems to be there. A drop over Christmas. And almost no vehicles were counted for a few days in July 2024. That's the data quality issue that was mentioned earlier, the sensors might not have been working. We will ignore that too for this tutorial.

 

Time series forecasting

Split the data into training and testing. We want a 7-day forecast. Hence put the most recent 7 days aside, to get a feel for the forecasting accuracy.

train_hdf = data_hdf.head(data_hdf.count()-7)
test_hdf = data_hdf.tail(7)

 

Train a time-series model on the training data. The AdditiveModelForecast algorithm is an implementation of Prophet. For simplicity no hyperparameter tuning is done here.

from hana_ml.algorithms.pal.tsa.additive_model_forecast import AdditiveModelForecast
amf = AdditiveModelForecast()
amf.fit(data=train_hdf, key='MEASUREDATE')

 

Investigate the model in a canned report.

from hana_ml.visualizers.unified_report import UnifiedReport
UnifiedReport(amf).build().display()

 

Apply the model on the test data, that was put aside earlier.

predicted_hdf = amf.predict(data=test_hdf)
predicted_hdf.head(5).collect()

 

Join the true values from the past with the prediction to assess the forecast accuracy.

predicted_hdf = predicted_hdf.set_index('MEASUREDATE').join(test_hdf.set_index('MEASUREDATE'))
predicted_hdf.head(5).collect()

 

Compare the actuals and prediction in a plot. The forecast is more accurate, than it might appear at first. Note how the y-axis is not starting at zero, but at 1.2 million. Hence the actuals and predictions are quite close.

import pandas as pd
predicted_df = predicted_hdf.collect()
predicted_df["VEHICLECOUNT"] = pd.to_numeric(predicted_df["VEHICLECOUNT"])
fig = px.line(predicted_df, x="MEASUREDATE", y=["VEHICLECOUNT","YHAT"] , title='Vehicle Count Forecast Accuracy test on known past')
fig.show()

 

Calculate the accuracy of the prediction. A Mean Absolute Percentage Error of 3.3% seems quite all right.

from hana_ml.algorithms.pal.tsa.accuracy_measure import accuracy_measure
metric_names = ['mape', 'rmse']
metrics = accuracy_measure(predicted_hdf.select(['VEHICLECOUNT', 'YHAT']), evaluation_metric=metric_names).collect()
metrics

 

Databricks contains a very comprehensive Repository where such model metrics (or hyperparameters, images, etc...) can be saved. Here we store just the absolute basics. For a much more advanced approach see this blog  "Developing HANA ML models with SAP Databricks" by @nidhi_sawhney.

import mlflow
with mlflow.start_run():
    for index, single_metric in metrics.iterrows():
        mlflow.log_metric(single_metric.STAT_NAME, single_metric.STAT_VALUE)

 

Now that we have a feeling for how well the model worked on the recent past, train a new model on the full history of the data.

from hana_ml.algorithms.pal.tsa.additive_model_forecast import AdditiveModelForecast
amf = AdditiveModelForecast()
amf.fit(data=data_hdf, key='MEASUREDATE')

 

Obtain list of the future 7 days that we want to predict. The historic data finishes on 18 January 2026.

futuredatestopredict_hdf = amf.make_future_dataframe(data_hdf, key='MEASUREDATE', periods=7)
futuredatestopredict_hdf.head(10).collect()

 

 

Apply the model to predict these future dates.

predictedfuture_hdf = amf.predict(data=futuredatestopredict_hdf, key='MEASUREDATE')
predictedfuture_hdf.head(10).collect()

 

Turn any potential negative predictions to zero. For this use case negative values would not make sense. Even though this very forecast doesn't have any negative values, you might need this step in your own projects. 

predictedfuture_hdf = predictedfuture_hdf.select('MEASUREDATE', ('GREATEST(YHAT, 0)', 'YHAT'),
                                                ('GREATEST(YHAT_LOWER, 0)', 'YHAT_LOWER'),
                                                ('GREATEST(YHAT_UPPER, 0)', 'YHAT_UPPER'))
predictedfuture_hdf.head(10).collect()

 

You can rename the columns, if you prefer, before writing the forecast to a table.

predictedfuture_hdf = predictedfuture_hdf.rename_columns(['MEASUREDATE', 'VEHICLECOUNT_PRED', 'VEHICLECOUNT_PREDUPPER', 'VEHICLECOUNT_PREDLOWER'])
predictedfuture_hdf.head(10).collect()

 

And with that, save the predictions as table to SAP Datasphere / SAP HANA Cloud.

predictedfuture_hdf.save("LOGISTICS_FORECAST", force=True)

 

Using the forecast in SAP Datasphere

The forecast is created and saved to a table. You can now integrate the information into your modelling and share with your users through SAP Analytics Cloud, or any other way you might prefer.

 

Summary

Now know in detail how SAP Databricks and native Databricks can trigger Machine Learning in SAP Datasphere. It's an option to consider to avoid data movement. SAP HANA Cloud has a long list of built-in algorithms. If there is one specific algorithm you are missing, just get in touch. Maybe it is there under a different name. If it really isn't there, get in touch to check with the Product Group whether it can be implemented.

Happy predicting!

entry.title: SAP UX Q1/2026 Update – Part 4: SAP SuccessFactors (AI, Joule and More)
entry.content:
This fourth post in my series gives an overview of user experience innovations in SAP SuccessFactors. The major UX highlight is the new home page design. Beyond that, we’ll look at examples of good UX in applications, new Joule, AI, and analytics capabilities helping users become more effective, and enhanced integration into Microsoft Teams.

Before reading this, do read the first blog post introducing the series and providing general information about Joule, SAP Build Work Zone and SAP SuccessFactors Work Zone, and SAP Mobile Start, along with links to all the published parts of this blog post series:

  • SAP UX Q1/2026 Update – Part 1: AI, Joule, SAP Build Work Zone, SAP Mobile Start.

Also, if you missed it, do have a look at the many UX innovations which already came out with the SAP SuccessFactors 1H2025 release:

  • SAP UX Q3/2025 Update – Part 4: SAP SuccessFactors (AI, Joule and More).

Our strategy for user experience has three aspects for helping users save time and effort, and helping their business run better:

  • Enjoyable: providing a modern, accessible user experience, consistent and integrated, with good usability.
  • Better: helping users achieve better outcomes with AI and embedded analytics.
  • Together: helping users work together in teams.

In this post, I have picked the following highlights from the latest SAP SuccessFactors 2H2025 release showcasing each of these aspects:

Enjoyable: design and usability enhancements
o.  New Home Page design (web and mobile) and WalkMe update.
o.  Examples of good UX in Workforce Management.

Better outcomes with AI and Analytic
o.  Career and Talent Development for employees and managers: skills, succession.
o.  Performance and Goals: Joule agent, AI and analytics for managers.
o.  Additional Joule support for employee self-services

Together: supporting collaboration in teams
o.  Joule integration into Microsoft Teams.
o.  AI-assisted quick writing available im Microsoft Teams

 

Enjoyable: Design and Usability Enhancements

The new home page design for the product will benefit all users. When it comes to specific applications, the 2H2025 release has a huge number of great enhancements, so it wasn’t easy to pick ones to highlight. I have chosen to include some of the innovations in Workforce Management. At the end of the blog post you’ll find links to all the news for the 2H2025 release.

New Home Page Design (Web)

The new Home Page design not only looks better, it also gives users tangible benefits, by becoming more intuitive and efficient, streamlining navigation and supporting personalization.

As you can see in Figure 1, the new design is cleaner and more attractive. The top navigation row contains Quick Actions, more compact than before. Below that the greeting section is new, with quick links and cards featuring timely and relevant information going beyond the user’s workflow actions and to-dos.

Figure 1: New Home Page for SAP SuccessFactors. Alt Text: Screenshot showing all the sections described in the text.

As you can see in Figure 1, we have separated Quick Actions and the Quick Links: the Quick Actions row at the very top allow users to quickly make updates or look at information while staying on the home screen: clicking on one of the Quick Actions opens a pop-over in which the user can do the action. Figure 2 shows two examples, where the user has chosen the Manage My Team and Give Feedback Quick Actions.

Figure 2: Quick Actions in the new home page. Alt Text: two screenshots each showing pop-overs for quick actions for (1) Manage My Team, with a list of team members on the left and two columns with links to actions, in groups “Change”, “View” and “Other actions”, (2) Give Feedback, with fields for the person, the topic and three questions (what went well, what to improve, additional comments).

Greeting Section: this section contains Quick Links on the left, that take you directly to the respective applications. On the right, the system displays cards featuring timely and relevant information that extend beyond the user’s workflow actions and to-dos.

Needs Attention: As before, tasks, to-dos, and workflow actions are available on the home page, but now with the improved design it is more intuitive and easier to use. The Needs Attention section provides a stack of cards for each type of task, to-do or action. They are displayed in just one row, within a carousel. If you have more than one card in the stack, the “View All” link opens a pop-over where you can see and interact with all the cards. Figure 3 shows an example, where the user has clicked on “View All (5)”  in the leftmost card (shown in Figure 1) representing “Compensation Planning”.

Figure 3: Needs Attention “View All” example, showing all tasks of type “Remind a team member to complete pending tasks”. Alt Text: Screenshot of a pop-over showing a card for each of the five team members, each with the task type as title and a “Send Email” button.

Organizational Updates: This section is for links to organizational information, including custom cards for company initiatives or messages. As you can see, we have standardized the card pattern and display information using only the smaller-sized cards here.

Explore More: this is where customers have more freedome to place data visualization and banner cards. With the 2H2025 we provide two analytic cards that can be made available there: Time Sheet Status and Upcoming Absences. Find out more in the

  • Documentation: Analytical Cards on the Latest Home Page.

Personalization: at the very bottom of the home page (see Figure 1), you have a “Personalize your home page experience” button to do that, via a pop-over (Figure 4). You can personalize the selection of Quick Actions, Quick Links and also choose whether you want your name to appear in the Greeting section or not.

Figure 4: Personalization of the home page. Alt Text: A screenshot of the home page with a popover “Home Page Settings” with three tabs “Greeting”, “Quick Links” (selected), and “Quick Actions”, and a list of links to choose from (Time Sheet, Team Absence Calendar,…), all currently selected.

Watch it in action and find out more about the new home page for web and mobile here:

  • Video: Meet the New Homepage & Mobile Experience in SAP SuccessFactors – 2H 2025 Release (3:10 minutes)
  • SAP SuccessFactors HCM: Updated Home Page 2H 2025
  • More design backround: Exciting updates are coming to your SAP SuccessFactors home page!

New Home Page Design (Mobile)

We have also updated the SAP SuccessFactors mobile app correspondingly (Figure 5). You can see the Quick Actions along the top of the Home screen, above the Needs Attention section. To-Dos are on a separate screen, divided into two tabs Approvals and Other, with a section for each type of To-Do. Filters at the top of the screen let you quickly get to those To-Dos you are most interested in at the moment.

Figure 5: New Home and To-Do design for SAP Success Factors mobile app. Alt Text: Two mobile screens, on the left the Home screen, with Quick Actions at the top, then “Needs Attention” and “Featured” sections, each with a row of cards. On the right the To-Do screen, with Approve and Other tabs, above filters for “My Team”, “Learning Assignments”,…, and sections “My Team (7)” and “Learning Assignments (4)”, each with one row of cards.

Easy On-Boarding with WalkMe

The new home page is intuitive, so that most users should be able to adapt to it easily. To ensure that all users feel comfortable with it, we have adapted the WalkMe onboarding tour to explain how to use the new home page. The short video in Figure 6 shows what this looks like:

Figure 6: WalkMe for the new home page. Alt Text: A video showing WalkMe appearing as an overlay on the home page, starting with an “Explore updated home page” pop-over, followed by pop-overs next to each explained feature.

Examples of Good UX in Workforce Management

I’d like to highlight the new capabilities for workforce scheduing and geofencing for time tracking.

SAP SuccessFactors Workforce Scheduling

The brand-new shift scheduling solution SAP SuccessFactors Workforce Scheduling is for companies that need to plan their production workforce schedules. It enables demand-driven workforce planning, supporting both weekly and monthly shift plans that account for operational requirements, employee skills, and labor regulations.

As you can see in Figure 7, it makes it easy for users (schedulers) to get visibility into workforce availability, allowing them to minimize downtime, increase productivity, and better align labor supply with business demand.

Figure 7: Workforce scheduling. Alt Text. Screenshot of a schedule with a list of people on the left and a calendar on the right with rows corresponding to the people, showing who is allocated to which shift on which day. The screenshot shows a shift being assigned, with a date-picker popover.

Geofencing in SAP SuccessFactors Time Tracking

In this release, customers can leverage a new geofencing feature that helps ensure employees clock in and out only when physically present at their designated work location. Geofences can be defined for specific groups, with real-time visibility into proximity, helping to improve compliance with location-based policies and prevent time recording outside authorized areas.

Figure 8: Geofencing. On the left: defining the geofences. On the right: what it looks like for a user clocking time on a smart phone. Alt Text: Two screenshots: on the left the app Manage Geofences with a list of locations on the left, and on the right for the selected one a map with areas marked with a circle, a triangle and a polygon. On the right a mobile screen for clocking time, showing the user’s location within the marked circle on a map, and a “clock time” button.

Read more about these in this blog post:

  • Second Half 2025 Release: What's new in SAP SuccessFactors solutions for Workforce Management

 

Better outcomes with AI and Analytics

SAP SuccessFactors continues to invest heavily AI and analytics to help users work more effectively with a better overall experience. I have chosen to highlight how employees benefit when planning their careers and their further development, how employees and managers benefit when they need to evaluate performance and goals, and how Joule is making employee self-services easier:

  • Career and Talent Development for employees and managers: skills, succession
  • Performance and Goals: Joule agent, AI and analytics for managers
  • Additional Joule support for employee self-services

Career and Talent Development for employees and managers: skills, succession

Automatically Create Skills from Resume

A system, or for that matter a manager, can only help people grow in the direction they would like if they know what skills a person has, and what development goals they aspire to reaching. This means you need to have people’s skills maintained in SAP SuccessFactors in order for the system to guide employees and managers.

People often have a wide variety of skills, so manually maintaining the full list of their skills is time-consuming and can mean that people don’t bother – resulting in a lack of data in the system.

Now, the 2H2025 release uses AI to make this much easier: users can simply attach their resume, and the system will extract skills from there automatically. Figure 9 shows an example.

Figure 9: Using AI to extract skills from your resume. Alt Text: Screenshot of the Growth Portfolio app with an “Add from Resume” pop-over, showing the file name Resume.pdf and a list of 16 skill groups, such as “Agile Methodology”, “Communication Skills”, each with a check box for selecting to add or not.

Once the skills have been created, you will want to be able to easily search through them or filter them – which is why we have introduced search and filtering to the skills page, as shown in Figure 10.

Figure 10: Search and filter your skills. Alt Text: Screenshot of Growth Portfolio app with a filter bar at the top with a search field and filter fields for Proficiency Level, Categories, Tags, and Time Period. The page shows three rows of six cards, each representing a skill with a horizontal chart shosing the current level of proficiency on a 1 to 5 scale.

AI-Recommended Development Goals Based On Skills

Now that you have your full set of skills maintained in the system you can start using SAP SuccessFactors to guide your growth. This is an important enabler of our goal to empower employees to take control of their professional development.

After maintaining your development goals, the 2H2025 release of SAP SuccessFactors uses AI to make recommendations for suitable learning courses and available assignments relevant to achieving your goals. Figure 11 shows an example.

Also new: development goals can now be transferred between multiple jobs held by the same person, ensuring continuity in their growth within the company.

Figure 11: AI recommendations for learning and assignments based on skills and development goals. Alt Text: Screenshot of Goal Details, showing a card for Linked Assignments above the main section Opportunities to Grow, containing two cards: one for Learning, one for an Assignment. Each card has an image, title and a few details.

AI and Analytics for Managing Career and Talent Development

People Intelligence, our new people analytics application, is now generally available. It is built on SAP Business Data Cloud, and fully integrated in SAP SuccessFactors. It offers intuitive dashboards, AI-assisted insights supported by Joule, and hundreds of HR metrics. To help customers get started, the application includes nine pre-built use cases, each for a specific HR domain.

One of these pre-built use cases is Skills Insights. It helps to identify existing skill strengths and gaps in the organization, to compare current skills to target proficiency levels, and to align workforce capabilities with your organization’s priorities. Figure 12 shows an example of the overview page, from where you can drill down into further detailed views.

Figure 12: An example overview page of Skills Insights. Alt Text: Screenshot showing various charts (line chart, horizontal bar charts, heat map) for Skills Adoption Overview, Employees with Skills, Most Adopted Skills, Proficiency Overview.

Find out more about Skills Insights in:

  • Documentation: Skills Insights.

Career Development Insights is another of the pre-built use cases. Figure 13 shows an example of the development goals page that offers insights into participation rates, alignment with learning and critical skills, and organizational progress.

Figure 13: An example of the development goals within Career Development Insights. Alt Text: Screenshot showing a panel on the left with filters, on the right two sections: Summary, with numerical KPIs; Details, with four horizontal bar charts and a donut chart showing number of employees with goals, goal status, most and least popular skills.

Find out more about Career Development Insights in:

  • Documentation: Career Development Insights.

AI Successor Recommendation Based On Skills

Another example of how SAP SuccessFactors uses AI to give people a better user experience is the new skill-based successor recommendations capability. This uses AI to identify who would be best suited to be your successor, by matching skill sets and attributes to the specific role requirements. Figure 14 shows what it looks like.

By analyzing skill proficiency levels and internal work experience in the employee growth portfolio, it recommends potential successors who might have been overlooked using traditional metrics.

Figure 14: AI successor recommendation based on skills. Alt Text: Screenshot of Succession app showing org chart view for manager John Williams with 3 successors underneath his image. There is a pop-over for John, with details of the position, and a list of three other suggested successors. For the first one, Michael Pittman, a further pop-over “Role Readiness Explanation” is shown listing five matched skills and competencies and four unmatched ones.

Performance and Goals: Joule Agent, AI and Analytics for Managers

When the time arrives for managers to review the performance of their team members, they typically need to reserve a considerable amount of time in getting an overview of each person’s activities, achievements, and feedback they have received.

Now, managers can save a lot of time by using the new Performance and Goals Agent within the SAP SuccessFactors Performance and Goals solution. Using Joule, managers get AI-guided insights – for example, into their areas of strength, areas needing improvement, goal progress summaries, key accomplishments, and development recommendations. Figure 15 shows an example.

Figure 15: Performance and Goals Agent. Alt Text: Joule in full screen mode, with a prompt “I have to meet with Christine Dolan about her performance. What should I know about her areas of strength and what she needs to improve?”. Below that the reply in two sections “Areas of Strength” and “Areas Needing Improvement”, containing bullet points.

The UI for managers to enter their assessment is intuitive, as shown in Figure 16. Now, with the 2H2025 release, HR departments can define business rules that trigger actions for users based on what they have entered. For example, a rule might stipulate that a low rating requires a comment to be entered, resulting in the comment field only being exposed when a user actually gives such a rating.

Have a look at many more examples of how the agent helps managers:

  • Video: Introducing the Performance & Goals AI Agent in SAP SuccessFactors (3:27 minutes). 

Also helpful for managers is the enhancement of the AI-powered Writing Assistant that is now available: they can use it to help formulate comments for performance ratings, as shown in Figure 16, and in the video linked above.

Figure 16: AI Writing Assistant for formulating comments when rating performance, embedded in the intuitive performance review UI. Alt Text: Screenshot of Performance feedback app, showing a list of goals with manager and employee rating, and a panel on the right with evaluation details for the goal “Ensure production documentation complies with standards”. The comments field is in focus, with a popover “Generate Comments” listing suggested texts – “Regularly review and update documentation” is highlighted.

Performance Insights

People Intelligence also comes with pre-built use cases for the performance and goals area, giving managers insights into performance metrics across the organization. Figure 17 shows an example of an overview.

Figure 17: Performance Overview in Performance Insights provided by People Intelligence. Alt Text: Screenshot with three sections Overview (showing KPIs), and Performance Distribution and Performance Rating Changes, containing a line chart, horizontal bar charts and a donut chart.

Find out more about what’s new for Performance and Goals here:

  • Second Half 2025 Release: What's new for SAP SuccessFactors Performance & Goals?
  • Documentation: Performance Insights.

Additional Joule support for employee self-services

Employees typically only rarely interact with the HR system, so they are casual users, who especially need an easy to understand, intuitive user experience. Joule helps here enormously, since users can just state what they want and Joule will help.

With the 2H2025 release, we have added further Joule actions. Users can now upload files directly through Joule in the flow of supported Joule scenarios, view organizational information of themselves or others, view extended time-off data, and create half-day leave requests based on scheduled working time. Figure 18 shows examples for a half day leave request and for viewing time off guidelines.

The “explain my pay” feature, that I highlighted in my previous UX blog post on SAP SuccessFactors, is now also available for employees in Mexico and in Germany – in their respective languages (Spanish and German). Figure 18 shows an example for Mexico.

Figure 18: Further Joule actions available now. Alt Text: three images of Joule: 1) with prompt “I wann apply half day leave tomorrow morning”, with Joule showing time off request; 2) “what is the time off guideline”, with Joule summarizing time off types and purposes; 3) “Explique mis deducciones”, with Joule providing a pay explanation in Spanish.

 

Together: Supporting Collaboration in Teams

Users working with Microsoft Teams can chat directly with Joule from within Teams, as well as benefitting from AI-assisted quick writing directly in-place.

Joule integration into Microsoft Teams

Users can now access chat with Joule while using Microsoft 365 Copilot within Microsoft Teams, as shown in Figure 19.

Figure 19: Access Joule from Microsoft Teams via Microsoft 365 Copilot. Alt Text: A screen showing Copilot open in Teams, where the user has typed in “show my leave requests”.

AI-assisted Writing Available in Microsoft Teams

Furthermore, AI-assisted writing is now built into the SAP SuccessFactors quick actions that are available to users in Microsoft Teams, such as giving feedback or creating activities. This helps users communicate more clearly and consistently, and save them time in formulation too. Figure 20 shows an example for giving feedback directly from within Teams.

Figure 20: AI-assisted writing in Microsoft Teams. Alt Text: A screenshot of Microsoft Teams showing Quick Actions for SAP SuccessFactors behind a pop-over for giving feedback, with some text entered into the “What went well?” question and a context menu pop-over listing the writing assistant menu items, with “Enhance Writing” in focus.

 

Further Information on 2H2025 Release Highlights

These four assets give you an overview of the overall release highlights for 2H2025, in increasing level of detail:

  • Video: SAP SuccessFactors 2H 2025 Release Highlights (3:16 min.)
  • Second Half 2025 Release: What’s New in SAP SuccessFactors HCM?
  • SAP SuccessFactors 2H 2025 Release: What’s New & What Matters.
  • SAP SuccessFactors HCM 2H 2025 Release Highlights Brochure.

The following posts give details of innovations in the 2H2025 release in these areas:

  • Employee Central.
  • Employee Central Payroll (ECP).
  • Learning.
  • Career and Talent Development.
  • Performance & Goals.
  • Workforce Management.
  • Mobile.

Finally, for all the details, have a look at the documentation:

  • SAP SuccessFactors What’s New Viewer.

 

Continued soon…

I hope you enjoyed this fourth part, covering SAP SuccessFactors, with great new user experiences helping employees and managers work more efficiently and effectively.

Part five is coming soon, covering latest UX innovations for managing your supply chain with SAP Integrated Business Planning.

Part 1 of the series lists all the parts, and links to those parts that have already been published:

  • SAP UX Q1/2026 Update – Part 1: AI, Joule, SAP Build Work Zone, SAP Mobile Start.

Do keep posting your experiences and recommendations yourself in the SAP Community, with the SAP Fiori and/or the User Experience tag! In case you are wondering how to get a list of the most recent blog posts on SAP Fiori and User Experience in our new SAP Community, use these links:

  • Latest blog posts on SAP Fiori in the SAP Community.
  • Latest blog posts on User Experience in the SAP Community.

entry.title: Re: Project Resource Demand - Service Activity Type Actua...
entry.content:
Hi @S0025758586

What i tried to explain is that there is no separate ML like automatic revaluation layer for service activity consumption on WBS. The standard path you should follow is to calculate actual activity rates on cost center, based on your business rule, then use allocation mechanisms to redistribute our adjust at period end.

On your question if there is a dedicated project valuation process with calculated actual price?

The simple answer is No. PC 2602 does not provide that in the way you mentioned. Which is why i made the recommendation to calculate actual rates and distribute or adjust to WBS earlier.

Best regards

Chris

PS: always use the @ sign to ensure follow-ups get to the intended person.

entry.title: Re: Custom Number Range for Business Partner while doing...
entry.content:
Your understanding is correct. In Customer–Vendor Integration (CVI), the Business Partner (BP) number can either:

  • Be the same as the existing Customer/Vendor number, or
  • Be assigned from an internal BP number range (system‑generated).

The CVI framework does not provide a mechanism to automatically transform or prefix numbers (e.g., adding “1000” or “2000” to existing numbers).

BP number assignment is strictly controlled by the BP number range configuration in S/4HANA.

Implication for your case

  • Since Customers and Vendors currently overlap in ECC (both using ranges like 100000–199999 and 100000–200000), you cannot directly reuse those numbers in S/4HANA for BPs without conflict.
  • To meet the client’s requirement (Customer BPs: 1000100000–1000199999, Vendor BPs: 2000100000–2000200000), you will need to:
    • Define new external BP number ranges with the desired prefixes.
    • Perform mapping/migration logic during CVI conversion to assign the new BP numbers.
    • This typically requires data migration tools (e.g., SAP Migration Cockpit, LSMW, or custom programs) to load BPs with the prefixed numbers, since CVI itself won’t add prefixes.

entry.title: Re: SAP BTP-CPI-Package-Domain-Centric-Pattern (PDCP):Solving Package Sprawl at Scale-PART II
entry.content:
@vinaymittal thanks for the technical scrutiny. Let's look at the actual math of the DCRP/PDCP blueprint:

1. The 5-Minute Blitz & TCO
Velocity: Because I’ve mastered the logic, I recently deployed 4 proxies across 4 distinct domains using dynamic routing for 10 subprocesses each.
Scale: This allows receiving messages from 40 different vendors via only 4 proxies, fully configured in just 5 minutes vs maybe 15 or 20 adding policies, my solution provides stable template, plugin and play javascript in any APIM without extra coding, just need to follow the logic, KVM + JavaScript.
New vendors comming ? Change KVM string, takes 10 secunds, ready to use.
TCO: The framework delivers $280K in total value—$50K labor, $30K infra, and $200K in risk mitigation—by optimizing the SAP APIM licenses you already own.
2. Performance & Edge Cases
Validation: Stress-tested with 30,000+ messages, maintaining <100ms latency at a 99.92% success rate.
Hotfixes: iFlow DNA (7-segment naming) ensures individual iFlows remain granular and redeployable within consolidated PDCP packages.
Security: The DCRP Engine extracts "vendor" at runtime, allowing conditional PCI/Security policies only where needed, providing more control than traditional 1:1 proxies.
Established 1:1 patterns create "unmanageable graveyards of objects." This framework replaces manual labor with architectural intelligence.

Think that’s pushing it? Wait until Chapter 3: The Commander’s Blueprint to end chaos globally within SAP BTP Integration Suite.

Best regards,

Ricardo Viana Enterprise Integration Architect | The Commander

entry.title: Re: I want to confirm the value of LASTCHANGE_DATE in the...
entry.content:
Recommended approach would be

  1. Test in your ECC system: Check a sample of work centers, make changes, and confirm whether LASTCHANGE_DATETIME updates reliably.
  2. If consistent, you can safely use it for incremental extraction.
  3. If inconsistent, consider alternative fields like AEDAT (Last Change Date) or use CDHDR/CDPOS (Change Document tables) for guaranteed change tracking.

entry.title: Re: When flagging the pricing condition for deletion a se...
entry.content:
Why both records are flagged because

  • Condition records are identified by a combination of key fields (condition type, key combination, validity dates, etc.).
  • When you originally created the record with validity starting 01.01.2026 → 31.12.9999, then later adjusted it to start from 01.05.2026, SAP internally still treats the “infinite” record as part of the same condition record chain.
  • Because the system sees both records as belonging to the same logical condition (same condition type, same key, same price), the deletion flag propagates to the related record.
  • This is especially common when the condition type is a copy of PR00 (pricing condition), since PR00 logic enforces uniqueness of condition records per key combination.

Key Points to Check

Condition Record Key: Verify the key combination (customer/material, etc.) — if both records share the same key, SAP considers them part of the same condition.

Validity Interval Splitting: When you shortened the validity of the “infinite” record, SAP may have created overlapping or split records internally. Deletion can cascade across these linked records.

Deletion Flag Behavior: The flag is not just applied to the record you select; it applies to all records that SAP considers duplicates (same key + same condition value).

How to handle this is if you want to delete only one validity interval, you may need to adjust the validity dates rather than flagging for deletion. Alternatively, create a new condition record with a different key (e.g., different condition table or access sequence) to separate them. Check with transaction VK12 or VKM3 to see how SAP is grouping these records.

entry.title: Upcoming Espresso Session-The Hidden Crisis: Mastering Asset Data to Solve the Ageing Infrastructure
entry.content:
Join us for a focused 30‑minute Espresso Session where SAP and PiLog unpack the “Data Problem No One Is Solving.” Learn why ageing infrastructure and reactive O&M are creating a silent emergency in Energy & Natural Resources — and how high‑quality, governed master data is the missing foundation for successful Digital Transformation, APM, and RISE with SAP.

 

 


What this session is about

  • Explain the “Data Wall”: why poor asset records block predictive maintenance, safety, and cloud ERP outcomes.
  • Show PiLog’s AI‑first, standards‑based approach and SAP‑certified integrations that create a “Clean Core” of trusted asset data.
  • Demonstrate how standardized asset models and pre‑built transformations de‑risk migrations and unlock APM, IBP for MRO, and AI use cases.

Why attend

  • Learn how to move from reactive maintenance to governed, predictive operations.
  • See how standards and millions of asset models cut manual work and increase data consistency.
  • Understand practical steps to make your SAP environment AI‑ready and deliver faster ROI on cloud ERP and APM initiatives.

Who should join
Asset managers, maintenance leads, digital transformation and IT leaders, ERP/APM migration sponsors, data governance and MRO teams.

Speakers
Smitha Rayala — Senior Director, Partner Strategy — SAP Intelligent Asset Management, SAP
Dr. Imad Syed — CEO, PiLog Group

When
Wednesday, February 18, 2026 — 17:00 CET (30 minutes)

Read the blog
Register for the session

Reserve your spot — don’t miss this practical session on turning ageing assets into reliable engines of growth.

entry.title: SAP PO to SAP CI Migration - Simplifying File-Based Scenarios with SMB Adapter in SAP CI
entry.content:
Every enterprise today is actively working on SAP PO to SAP Cloud Integration (CI) migration as part of their move towards modern, cloud-based integration platforms. While many interface types can be migrated smoothly, one of the most common challenges during SAP PO to SAP Cloud Integration (CI) migration is handling file-based interfaces that rely on internal on-premise file systems. In many landscapes, business applications exchange data by placing files on shared network folders, and SAP PO integrations are tightly built around these locations.

In SAP PO, this was quite straightforward. The File/NFS Adapter allowed direct access to internal network paths without the need for FTP servers or external exposure. As a result, many legacy integrations continue to depend on this simple and secure approach.

But when moving to SAP Integration Suite, teams quickly realize an uncomfortable truth: SAP CI does not provide a native NFS adapter. Since SAP CI runs in the cloud, it cannot directly access internal file systems, making a direct “like-for-like” migration of such scenarios difficult.

This creates a big question:

How do we migrate scenarios where files are stored inside the customer’s internal network?

The Real-World Problem

  • Many SAP PO interfaces read/write files from internal network drives
  • These locations are usually:
    • Windows shared folders
    • Internal file servers
    • Application-generated file locations
  • Access happens inside the internal network and not over the internet

SAP PO and SAP CI Comparison:

AspectSAP POSAP CI
Access to Internal File SystemsNFS adapter could directly access internal network pathsNo native NFS adapter available
Additional InfrastructureNo need for FTP servers or external exposureCI runs on cloud which cannot directly access internal file systems
Security & SimplicitySecure and simple setup using internal mountsRequires alternative approach for on-prem file access
Migration ImpactFile-based scenarios worked seamlessly with NFS

Blocks a direct “like-for-like” migration from SAP PO

 

Solution:

Option 1 – FTP/SFTP

A common workaround suggested in projects is to introduce an FTP/SFTP server and move files there first.

But highlight why this is not ideal:

  • FTP is not recommended for security reasons
  • Requires extra infrastructure

So we need something better.

Option 2 – SMB Adapter (Best fit)

SAP has released the SMB Adapter in SAP Integration Suite specifically designed to address this exact problem.

The SMB adapter facilitates file-based operations and accelerates implementation time to connect to a server using SMB (Server Message Block) protocol. The adapter is available only for Standard service plans and above of the SAP Integration Suite.

Feature highlights:

  • Secure authentication with Server Message Block (SMB) shares.
  • Offers compatibility with various SMB dialects.
  • On-Premise Connectivity via Cloud Connector.
  • Supports connections to SMB using Encryption, DFS, and Signing.
  • Provides different file handling options such as override, append, fail, and ignore.

 

High-Level File Based Architecture Explanation:

The above diagram illustrates the architectural shift when migrating file-based integrations from SAP PO to SAP Cloud Integration.

In SAP PO landscapes (Diagram 1), accessing files stored inside the customer’s internal network was relatively simple. The SAP PO File Adapter could directly read and write files from network locations using the NFS protocol.

Application servers typically mounted shared drives, and SAP PO accessed them seamlessly as local folders.

However, in SAP Cloud Integration ((Diagram 2) this approach is no longer possible. Since SAP CI runs in the cloud, it cannot directly mount or access internal network file systems. Moreover, SAP Integration Suite does not provide an NFS adapter like SAP PO.

This creates a major migration challenge for scenarios where:

  • Business applications drop files on internal network folders
  • SAP PO polls those folders using the File/NFS adapter
  • No FTP/SFTP servers are involved

To address this gap, SAP introduced the SMB Adapter in SAP Integration Suite.

The lower part of the diagram shows the modern CI-based approach:

  • Internal folders are exposed as SMB Shares on the customer network
  • SAP Cloud Connector establishes a secure tunnel between SAP CI and the on-premise network
  • The SMB Adapter in SAP CI connects through Cloud Connector
  • Communication happens over TCP using the SMB protocol
  • Files can be securely read from or written to internal locations without exposing them to the internet

This architecture provides a clean and secure replacement for NFS-based integrations in SAP PO, enabling a smooth migration path for file-based scenarios without introducing FTP servers or additional middleware.

Additional Scenario – When an SMB Server Already Exists

In many customer landscapes, an SMB file server is already available in the internal network (diagram 1), and applications directly place files on SMB shared folders. In SAP PO setups, even in such cases, the File/NFS adapter cannot consume these files directly. The SMB share first needs to be mounted on the SAP PO server and exposed as a local file path. Only after this mounting, SAP PO can access the location using the NFS/File adapter. This adds additional administrative effort and dependency on server configurations.

With SAP Cloud Integration, this limitation no longer exists. The SMB Adapter in SAP CI can connect directly to an existing SMB server through SAP Cloud Connector without any need for drive mounting. As long as the folder is available as an SMB share and properly exposed via Cloud Connector, SAP CI can immediately read from or write to it. This significantly simplifies the architecture, reduces infrastructure dependencies, and makes the migration of such scenarios from SAP PO to SAP CI much more straightforward.

In the Next Sections, We Will See:

  1. How to : Create or update an SMB Share on the network
  2. Configure: Cloud Connector for SMB access
  3. Build: Configure SMB Adapter in SAP CI
  4. Demo Scenario:
    • Pick a file from on-prem location
    • Process it in SAP CI
    • Delete the file as a part of post processing

Section A – Preparing the SMB Share

In our scenario, the internal file server is Linux-based. The following steps need to be executed by the Infrastructure team to create and expose a folder as an SMB share. These commands can be run on the Ubuntu server via SSH using tools like PuTTY or the command prompt.

# Update packages - before installing new components, ensure that the system packages are up to date
sudo apt update
 
# Install Samba and client tools required for SMB communication
sudo apt install samba smbclient -y
 
# Create the folder that will be exposed as an SMB share
sudo mkdir -p /srv/smb/smb_share
 
# Create SMB user
sudo adduser smbuser
 
# Set directory ownership and permissions
sudo chown -R smbuser:smbuser /srv/smb/smb_share
sudo chmod -R 775 /srv/smb/smb_share
 
# Backup Samba config
sudo cp /etc/samba/smb.conf /etc/samba/smb.conf.bak
 
# Configure the SMB Share - Add the new share definition to the Samba configuration
sudo bash -c 'cat >> /etc/samba/smb.conf <<EOF
 
[smb_share]
   path = /srv/smb/smb_share
   browseable = yes
   read only = no
   valid users = smbuser
   force user = smbuser
   create mask = 0660
   directory mask = 0770
EOF'
This configuration defines:
  • The physical path of the share
  • Read/write access
  • Authorized user
  • File and folder permission masks
# Create and enable the Samba-specific password for the SMB user:
sudo smbpasswd -a smbuser
sudo smbpasswd -e smbuser
 
# Restart Samba services
sudo systemctl restart smbd nmbd
 
# Allow Samba through firewall (if UFW is enabled)
sudo ufw allow samba
 
# Verify Samba configuration
testparm -s
 
# Verify share listing
smbclient -L localhost -U%
 
# Test share access
smbclient //localhost/smb_share -U smbuser

#Accessing the Share from Windows access: Once the SMB share is created, it can be accessed from any authorized Windows machine using the network path format using the credentials created above:
\\<server-name-or-ip>\smb_share

Deploy the credentials (smbuser) as basic credentials in SAP CI Security Artifact, these credentials will be configured in the SMB Adapter for authentication.

Section B – Cloud Connector Configuration

As we are connecting to On Premise Server, Cloud Connector comes into picture for the connectivity. Check out this article to setup TCP connection in Cloud Connector: https://help.sap.com/docs/connectivity/sap-btp-connectivity-cf/configure-access-control-tcp

 

Section C – Setup SMB Adapter in SAP CI Iflow

SAP CI IFlow:

 

 

Configuration of SMB Sender Adapter

 

Field

Description

Address

Specify the hostname or IP address of the SMB Server.

Specify Virtual Hostname if Cloud Connector is used

Domain

Specify the domain used for authentication.

Proxy Type

Specify the proxy type:

 • Internet • On-Premise

Location ID

Location ID of Cloud Connector (proxy type = On-Premise)

SMB Dialect

Select the SMB dialect or version to be used for communication.

An SMB dialect is a specific version of the SMB protocol that defines the supported features, capabilities, and communication formats between a client and a server

Share

Specify the SMB share folder which represents the network resource

Example: Assuming the mount path is \\ServerName\ShareName, you must specify Share as ShareName.

Authentication

Select the authentication type for connecting to the SMB server. Currently only Basic Authentication is supported

Credential Name

User Credentials security artifact

Timeout (in secs)

Specify the maximum waiting time (in seconds) for a response message from the SMB Server.

Enable Encryption

Enable to turn on encrypted communication between client & server.

This option is applicable only if the server supports it. This add encryption to the data in transit, supported only for SMB 3.x dialect

Enable DFS

Enable Distributed File System

DFS in SMB is a feature that provides a unified virtual file system namespace, allowing multiple SMB file shares across different servers to be accessed through a single logical path.

Enable Signing

Enable SMB Signing.

This option is applicable only if the server supports it. This adds signing to the data in transit. supported only for SMB 3.x dialect

Maximum Reconnect Attempts

Specify the maximum number of retries permissible for reconnection to the SMB server before message processing starts.

The processing tab contains the following fields:

This tab has parameters similar to what we see in SFTP Adapter Processing.

Please check this document for detailed information: https://api.sap.com/odata/1.0/catalog.svc/Files('2ee08d2557d84acfab3331448c2784b1')/$value

 

Section D - Demo:

In the Demo, the idea is to read a file from the SMB Share using SMB Sender Adapter.

SMB Share:

 

 

 

 

Content in the File:

The Sender Adapter Configuration is shown as above, Lets deploy the IFlow now.

Message run in TRACE mode:

File is received in SAP CI and deleted from the SMB Share.

In this similar manner, you can also use SMB receiver adapter to write a file on the folder, the connection tab configuration remains same as Sender Adapter, Operation Type in the Processing tab will be changed to “Create File”

Conclusion:

Migrating file-based scenarios from SAP PO to SAP CI no longer needs to be a roadblock.

The SMB Adapter provides a clean, secure, and SAP-recommended approach to connect SAP Integration Suite with internal network file systems making it the closest and most reliable replacement for the NFS adapter of SAP PO.

With minimal changes to existing infrastructure and no need for FTP servers, the SMB adapter truly simplifies one of the trickiest parts of PO to CI migration.

 

Hope this help,

Cheers!

Punith

entry.title: Re: Recommendation for Standard API / Function Module – Q...
entry.content:
Hi,

thank you for the details provided.

1.)  The referred function module - QB_VB_QINF_POST - is a non-released one, which means it is supported during SAP internal use cases only. If you plan to use it in a custom development, that can be done for own responsibility. For more information, see KBA:

1909989 - FAQ - Function Modules usage and status.

2.) A released module (BAPI) is not provided for quality information records (QINF).

3.) As of SAP S/4HANA 2020, there is an API available - API_QUALITYINFORECORD_SRV - to update QINF records, see the details below:

///API_Explorer///
'Quality Info Record'
https://api.sap.com/api/OP_API_QUALITYINFORECORD_SRV_0001/resource/Quality_Info_Record_Data?ReleaseInfo=2020%20FPS02

///SAPHelp///
'Quality Info Record'
https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_ON-PREMISE/a08e12a754cf4891b41a01a285d065bb/e568441aef594a4dba2f98ca53f4c85f.html

Have a nice day,

SAP Support

entry.title: Re: Create Subcontracting process
entry.content:
Why the error occurs is because

Recursive BOM: When the header and component are identical, SAP interprets this as a circular dependency. Subcontracting logic doesn’t allow the same material to be both supplied and consumed.

Serial Number Status: After the 541 movement, the serial number is in ESTO (stock at vendor). At GR, the system expects consumption of components and return of the repaired material. Because the component is the same as the header, the system cannot resolve the serial number status correctly.

Options to Handle Repair Subcontracting

1. Use a Dummy Component Approach

  • Create a “repair service” or dummy material as the component in the BOM.
  • This allows the subcontracting PO to be created without recursion.
  • The dummy component is consumed, while the serialized header material is issued (541) and received back (101).

2. Service PO Instead of Subcontracting PO

  • If no physical components are consumed, consider using a service PO for repair charges.
  • The serialized material can be sent to vendor with movement 541Q (stock provided to vendor) and received back with movement 101Q.
  • The service PO covers the repair cost, while the material movement handles the logistics.

3. Custom Process / Enhancements

  • Explore implementing user exits or BAdIs to bypass the BOM requirement for repair scenarios.
  • This avoids recursive BOMs and allows direct subcontracting of serialized items.

entry.title: Re: JMS Mastery Series Part 3 - JMS in Production
entry.content:
Hi Mateusz,

Big fan of JMS here too—though not so much of the pipeline concept as it’s currently delivered.

Scalability issues in that part of the cloud runtime (JMS) are often overlooked, and I find this to be a very interesting topic. If you want a truly scalable cloud platform, it will significantly affect the designs.

Doing “queue economy” and keeping things efficient while still providing proper monitoring for business users who are used to the old monitoring tools such as SAP PO, it´s often one of the hardest things to get right.

By the way, have you found any information on what a ‘valid justification’ means when it comes to escalating JMS queues? I’ve heard that it may also be related to the license version, but I still don’t have a clear understanding of when it’s possible to request this.

Thanks for the effort and for sharing this—it’s valuable.

entry.title: Re: Project stock and WBS element status CLOSED
entry.content:
Why you can close a WBS with Stock or Open Commitments is because of the following:

System Status vs. Business Rules:
The system status CLSD (Closed) on a WBS element only prevents new postings (e.g., no new actual costs, commitments, or stock movements). It does not check whether existing stock, open POs/PRs, or balances are zero.

Settlement Profile Not Relevant Here:
Settlement profile controls whether costs must be settled before technically completing or closing. Since your WBS has no settlement profile, there’s no system check for balances.

Valuated Project Stock:
Even with valuated project stock, SAP does not enforce “stock must be zero” before closing. The stock remains in MBBS, but you cannot issue or receive against the closed WBS anymore.

Open POs/PRs:
The system does not automatically block closing if commitments exist. Instead, once the WBS is closed, you cannot create new commitments or post goods receipts/invoices against it. The existing commitments remain in reporting until they are manually closed or deleted.

How SAP expects you to handle this

  • System Status CLSD is a technical lock, not a reconciliation check.
  • If your business requires “no stock, no open commitments” before closure, you need to enforce this via:
    • User Status profiles with checks (e.g., status check functions in OPSI).
    • Custom validations in user exits (e.g., CJ20N exit) to prevent closure if stock or commitments exist.
    • Process discipline: run MBBS/commitment reports before closing and clear balances.
